Using minimal fleet sizes, the PSVRP was found to reduce real-world costs by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50 - 58 \%$</tex> and emissions by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$39 - 46\%$</tex> compared to diesel fleets. The uniform distribution adequately approximated modeled real-world costs (within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6-8 \%$</tex>), whereas clustered distributions consistently underestimated costs and emissions. This study demonstrates the PSVRP's economic and environmental benefits in rural settings and highlights the ability of uniformly distributed theoretical scenarios to approximate real-world conditions.

title: Ultra-Wide GaAs Based Double Balanced MMIC Mixer for 50MHz to 2.8GHz Band
authors: Prashant, Harshita Tolani, Vinit Kumar, MukeshKumar K Patel, Ch.V.N. Rao
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents the design of an ultra-wideband (193%) double-balanced MMIC (Monolithic Microwave Integrated Circuit) mixer operating at 50 MHz to 2.8 GHz Radio Frequency (RF) frequency range. A transformer-based balun is employed to deliver differential signals to the diode ring structure enabling compact (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \text{mm} \times 2 \text{mm}$</tex>) and efficient design. It covers Local Oscillator (LO) frequency from 5–6 GHz. The proposed mixer achieves a conversion loss below 11.5 dB, with good isolation performance: RF-to-IF isolation greater than 35dB, LO-to-IF and LO-to-RF isolation exceeding 58dB, and return loss better than 10 dB across all three ports. The mixer design is carried out using a 250 nm pHEMT (pseudo-High Electron Mobility Transistor) GaAs process. This design caters to the development of backend receiver for Hyperspectral sounder which requires ultra-wide bandwidth from VHF (Very High Frequency) to S band and high isolation.

title: Multidimensional Supervised Learning over Big Data: Models, Definitions, and Solutions
authors: Alfredo Cuzzocrea
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Information Communication and Software Engineering (ICICSE)
abstract: Supervised Learning is an important task in Artificial Intelligence (AI) in various areas such as Computer Vision and Image Understanding, Data Mining (DM) and Knowledge Discovery, and so forth. In the era of big data, it faces critical challenges coming from the curse of dimensionality, heterogeneous data sources, and the need for scalable computation. To address these, Multidimensional Supervised Learning (MSL) has emerged as a formal paradigm that unifies multidimensional modeling with predictive analytics. This talk introduces theoretical foundations of MSL, along with rigorous definitions of multidimensional data, where facts, dimensions, hierarchies, and measures are explicitly represented to preserve structural and semantic richness. Our approach for performing MSL over big data builds upon OLAP-based multidimensional modeling to organize large-scale datasets into interpretable and computationally efficient structures. On top of this modeling layer, we perform pattern discovery and pattern matching across data hierarchies to capture meaningful relationships and enhance predictive accuracy. The workflow is completed with advanced visualization techniques, enabling intuitive exploration of discovered patterns and providing meaningful information to decision-makers. By formalizing definitions, developing models, and presenting scalable solutions, this paper positions multidimensional supervised learning as a basis for next-generation big data analytics.

title: A Wideband 3 × 3 Nolen Matrix Using Modified Coupler and Crossover for WLAN Applications
authors: Shrawan K Patel, Mrinal K Mandal
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: A microstrip line-based topology is presented for a Nolen matrix in PCB technology. It provides significant bandwidth improvements without increasing its size. A combination of newly developed modified directional coupler (DC) and a crossover offer at least 400% higher effective bandwidth compared to its conventional hybrid coupler (HC) based counterpart. A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times 3$</tex> Nolen matrix-based beamforming network using progressive phase differences of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0^{\circ}, 120^{\circ}$</tex> and - 120° is fabricated and tested. The approach is applicable to any higher-order matrix. Present fabricated prototype of matrix shows return loss <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>12 ~\text{dB}$</tex>, isolation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>20 ~\text{dB}$</tex>, amplitude imbalance of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 1.5 ~\text{dB}$</tex>, and phase error of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 10^{\circ}$</tex> over the band of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.15-2.65 \text{GHz}$</tex>, sufficient for wireless LAN applications. Overall circuit size of the present structure is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.93 \lambda^{2} g$</tex>.

title: A Simple Pipeline for 3D Object Detection and Motion Prediction Without Prior Information Through Semantic Understanding
authors: Haku Shinoda, Yunsoo Bok, Keisuke Yoneda, Keigo Hariya, Yukiya Fukuda, Naoki Suganuma
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Object detection and motion prediction of surrounding traffic participants are crucial for safe driving of autonomous vehicles. In general, numerous approaches for motion prediction rely on tracking information obtained through object detection and tracking process and improve prediction accuracy by utilizing high-definition (HD) maps. However, dependences on these elements can lead to a reduction in prediction performance in real-world driving scenarios, particularly in map-less environments or under tracking inaccuracies. As an alternative approach, we propose a simple pipeline for 3D object detection and motion prediction only using sequential LiDAR point clouds. The proposed method performs multi-task without prior information such as HD maps or tracking results, by extending PointPillars, a baseline 3D object detection network. Evaluations on our dataset show that detection accuracy of small objects such as cyclists and pedestrians improved by utilizing sequential point clouds. In motion prediction, the proposed method also achieved higher accuracy than the Constant Acceleration model across all the classes. In particular, Self-Attention Blocks and the MapSeg module respectively enhance temporal feature extraction and road structure learning, both contributing to improved prediction accuracy for moving objects.

title: Impact of 3G, 4G and 5G Mobile Phone Radiation on Brain and Reproductive System of Wistar Rat
authors: Kumari Vandana Singh, Rohit Gauta, Neha Jha, Jay Prakash Nirala, Paulraj Rajamani
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: Proliferation of cell phones has raised concerns regarding radio frequency radiation (RFR) exposure and its potential health effects on environment and human health. Young adolescents use it for longer durations, which exposes them to radiofrequency electromagnetic radiation (RFEMR). This exposure may lead to neuropsychiatric changes. The underlying cellular mechanism behind these changes requires detailed investigation. Hence the present study, we investigated the effect of RF-EMR emitted from mobile phones on Wistar rat. Wistar rats (young adolescent and old male and female) were exposed to 3G, 4G and 5G mobile phone radiation (specific absorption rate (SAR) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.51 ~\mathrm{W} / \text{kg}$</tex>) for different durations. Various biochemical assays such as catalase, glutathione peroxidase, super oxide dismutase, level of inflammatory cytokines, corticosterone and hippocampus dependent memory, DNA strand breaks and morphological alteration etc. were performed in rat exposed to different frequencies of microwave radiation. Results show that a significant changes in the level of proinflammatory cytokines, hypothalamic pituitary adrenal axis (HPA) activity and oxidative stress in hippocampus of exposed rats. Exposed group showed changes in reproductive parameters in male rat and female rat.

title: Coastal Zone Monitoring Based on Watercraft Motion Classification to Enhance Water Safety
authors: Tatiana Ya. Gladkikh, Konstantin D. Rusakov, Aleksey V. Grafenkov
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Information, Control, and Communication Technologies (ICCT)
abstract: A method for monitoring coastal water areas using video data analysis obtained from unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to enhance water safety is presented. The proposed approach involves detecting water transport objects in images using a modern deep learning model and classifying their movement (moving or stationary) based on optical flow. A dataset of over 2500 aerial images was developed and annotated, including vessels and their wake traces. A detection model with high accuracy (F1-score up to 0.98) was trained to identify vessels on water. For movement classification, a combination of optical flow analysis and wake trace detection is used; a composite metric is proposed to accurately distinguish moving vessels from stationary ones. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach for automated water area monitoring and early detection of potentially hazardous situations.

title: Multi-Objective Economic and Emission Dispatch Optimization using Grey Wolf Optimizer: A Case Study of the Ashuganj Power Plant, Bangladesh
authors: A.M. Tamim Haider
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: This study analyzes the effects of economic and emission dispatch on the Ashuganj Power Station Company Limited in Bangladesh, which consists of eight generating units. The three optimization formulations we used are Economic Load Dispatch (ELD) to minimize operating cost, Economic Emission Dispatch (EED) to minimize pollutants, and a Combined Economic and Emission Dispatch (CEED) to balance both objectives. The Grey Wolf Optimizer is used to compute the optimum dispatch values, while considering the unit limits, load demand, and simplifying assumptions such as all units would be online, the transmission line losses will be negligible, and the fuel prices are considered. We found that relative to ELD, EED cuts emissions by approximately 28.5% at 19.3% higher operating cost, whereas CEED achieves ~17.7% emission reduction for 4.3% additional cost (based on the study’s hourly results). The Pareto frontier spans approximately $24.44k–$29.16k per hour in cost and 2349 to 1680 ton/hr in emissions, with a clear "knee" near the CEED point (−17.7% emissions for +4.3% cost). By using the simplified assumption and using the optimizer to solve the dispatch problems, the trade-offs between the dispatches can be clearly identified which can help the decision-making in a given scenario.

title: Unmasking the Accuracy Illusion: Leveraging Gen AI for Enhanced Security in 6G O-RAN
authors: Kanwardeep Singh Gahlot, Sudhir Kumar, Sharda Tripathi, Sandeep Joshi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Networks and Telecommunications Systems (ANTS)
abstract: The evolution of Open Radio Access Networks (O-RANs) is reshaping wireless communications by enabling flexibility, virtualization, and multi-vendor interoperability. However, their disaggregated architecture broadens the attack surface, posing new security risks. Effective intrusion detection remains challenging due to emerging threats, limited labeled data, and class imbalance, which renders traditional metrics like accuracy unreliable. To address this, we propose Fense, a framework that combines feature-engineered Tabular Generative Adversarial Networks (TGANs) with Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) for robust performance evaluation and synthetic data generation. Experiments show that Generative AI (Gen AI) enhances detection accuracy, fairness, and resilience, tackling core challenges in O-RAN security.

title: Shockwave Minimization within the Mesoscopic: Infrastructure-Guided Optimal Acceleration for Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Minhee Kang, Yoojin Choi, Eunseo Choi, Heejin Ahn
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel mesoscopic shock-wave mitigation framework, uniquely leveraging infrastructure-guided acceleration commands to Connected Autonomous Vehicles (CAVs). We formulate a nonlinear optimization problem to minimize upstream shockwave propagation and speed deviations from free-flow. Notably, by integrating classical macroscopic traffic models (LWR and Greenshields) with microscopic vehicle dynamics (IDM and Krauss), we define “segment” as a mesoscopic concept in which infrastructure monitors individual vehicles (micro), computes average segment speed (macro), detects shockwaves, and transmits optimal reference accelerations to CAVs. We validate through simulations that our approach effectively mitigates shockwaves, maintains segment speed closer to free-flow, and reduces shockwave duration.

title: Detection Challenges in BB84 Quantum Key Distribution under Random Number Generator Compromise and QBER Analysis
authors: Shashwat Saxena, Anand Srivastava, Vimal Bhatia, Pravindra Kumar, Ritu Raj Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Networks and Telecommunications Systems (ANTS)
abstract: This paper investigates the compromised random number generator (CRNG) attack, a novel quantum hacking threat to the quantum key distribution (QKD) that compromises random number generators (RNGs) that are crucial for basis selection and key generation. These vulnerabilities at the device level are a complement to previous research on implementation and side-channel attacks against hardware used in quantum communication, which take advantage of real-world flaws rather than the quantum channel itself. By preventing an increase in the quantum bit error rate (QBER), CRNG makes undetected key recovery possible, in contrast to quantum channel attacks. In addition to the above investigation, two methods are highlighted in the paper, namely, parametric tampering and reconstruction, which are supported by a mathematical model. 100% key recovery at insignificant QBER is possible with totally compromised RNGs, according to simulations. The simulations also show how the effect of a noisy quantum channel diminishes the similarity between the recreated key and the legitimate key. The findings highlight the necessity for protections beyond channel monitoring, such as quantum RNGs and hybrid key generation methods, and reveal weaknesses in traditional QKD components.

title: Cross-component Transfer Fault Diagnosis via Joint Distribution Adaptation on Envelope Spectrum Features
authors: Yu Liu, Yaqiong Lv, Xiaohu Zhang, Nanyue Xiong, Jiding Yao, Jian Hao
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: In recent years, multiple domain adaptation methods have been proposed to improve the performance of fault diagnosis models on cross-component fault diagnosis tasks. However, most of these methods only focus on marginal distribution alignment or conditional distribution alignment between cross-domain fault features, ignoring the weight relationship between the two, resulting in the problem of poor performance of fault diagnosis models in target scenarios. In order to improve the fault diagnosis performance of the fault diagnosis model on cross-component tasks, this paper proposes a cross-component transfer fault diagnosis method via envelope spectrum features joint distribution adaptation. This method first uses Hilbert transform (HT) and fast Fourier transform (FFT) to construct the frequency domain envelope spectrum features of the raw signal. Then, in the cross-component fault diagnosis network, a new envelope spectrum joint distribution adaptation mechanism is introduced. By adjusting the weights of marginal distribution adaptation and conditional distribution adaptation, the domain alignment of the envelope spectrum between different components is achieved, and the fault diagnosis model's feature learning ability of the fault diagnosis model for faulty components in the target scenario is improved. Experiments show that the built cross-component transfer fault diagnosis network via envelope spectrum features joint distribution adaptive diagnosis accuracy of up to 93.5% on cross-component fault diagnosis task under the unlabeled conditions of target domain sample, proving the superior performance of the cross-component transfer fault diagnosis method proposed in this paper.

title: A Transferable Deep Learning Framework for Lithium-ion Battery State-of-Health Prediction Based on CL-Transformer
authors: Xuanang Gui, Tong Zhao, Junrong Du, Yuheng Cheng, Ruihua Yu, Junhua Zhao
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: The accurate prediction of State-of-Health (SOH) is fundamental to the operational reliability and lifespan of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in demanding applications, including electric vehicles and energy storage systems. This paper introduces a novel, transferable deep learning architecture, the CL-Transformer model, to address challenges in SOH estimation. Our proposed model synergistically combines a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to extract salient features, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to capture temporal dependencies, and a Transformer to model global sequence relationships. Empirical evaluation on the CALCE and NASA datasets shows that our model significantly outperforms existing single and hybrid models on RMSE, MAE, and R2 metrics. Furthermore, we validate the effectiveness of a loss item combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and Correlation Alignment (CORAL) in a transfer learning context to improve the model’s generalization ability. The importance of hyperparameter optimization for achieving peak performance is also highlighted. Our results confirm that the CL-Transformer model provides a robust framework for advancing the accuracy of battery SOH prediction.

title: Thermal Design of the Collector for Folded Waveguide TWT for THz Application
authors: Chirag Mistry, Amitavo Roy Choudhury
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: The folded waveguide structure is one of the promising structures for the millimeter and sub- millimeter frequency application. Due to the high operating voltage of the electron gun, thermal management plays an important role in the design of this Folded Waveguide Travelling Wave Tube (FW-TWT) for terahertz frequency applications. The performance and efficiency of the FW-TWT have direct relevance to the heat distribution and thermal equilibrium condition. Therefore, in these paper authors have presented thermal design and simulation of the FW-TWT collector using ANSYS tool for room temperature and different environment temperatures. The simulation result i.e. maximum temperature of the collector is validated analytically. The estimated maximum temperature of the FW-TWT collector under the worst-case scenario is below the allowable temperature limit of the material.

title: Certificateless Probabilistic Group Signature Verification Scheme Against Content Poisoning Attacks in NDN
authors: Atanu Sarkar, Sangram Ray, Mou Dasgupta
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Networks and Telecommunications Systems (ANTS)
abstract: Information Centric Networking (ICN) paradigm introduces a shift from a host-centric model to content-centric communication by enabling in-network caching and location-independent content retrieval. Among the various ICN architectures, Named Data Networking (NDN) and Content Centric Networking (CCN) have emerged as leading frameworks for the future Internet. Despite their advantages, NDN remains susceptible to Content Poisoning Attacks (CPA), where adversaries inject fake or harmful data into the network. These attacks compromise content integrity, authenticity, waste bandwidth, and decrease data delivery performance. Existing countermeasures typically rely on content signature verification at intermediate routers during forwarding. Although this ensures data authenticity, it imposes significant computation overhead, which adversely affects router performance and overall network efficiency. To overcome these limitations, we propose a trust-based certificateless group signature verification mechanism tailored for NDN. Trust values are dynamically calculated for content producers based on their history of successful signature verification. Further, we are enabling a probabilistic signature verification strategy that minimizes redundant verification operations. We integrate an identity-based signature scheme and a batch verification technique to reduce computation overhead. A verification module placed at the edge routers ensures efficient filtering of the content and avoids repetitive signature verification. Formal security proofs confirm the signature’s unforgeability of the proposed scheme and its robustness against CPAs. The ndnSIM simulation results demonstrate that our scheme achieves lower computation overhead and improved performance as compared to the existing state-of-the-art schemes.

title: PSRR-Aware (gm/Id)-Based LDO Design with Load-Capacitance and Quiescent-Current Optimization
authors: Sakshi Arora, R.R. Manikandan
publish: 2025 IEEE 6th International Women in Technology Conference (WINTECHCON)
abstract: This paper presents a detailed analytical model for power-supply rejection ratio (PSRR) in externally-compensated low-dropout regulators (LDOs), with emphasis on accurately capturing the non-ideal error-amplifier (EA) behavior. Leveraging this model, accurate estimation of the minimum-required load capacitance ($C_{L}$) and quiescent current ($I_{Q}$) is done, thereby preventing overdesign and reducing area and power overhead. Unlike previous works that assumed unity EA-PSRR, this work introduces two small-signal parameters, $\alpha$ and $\beta$, to accurately capture the DC and high-frequency PSRR behavior of the EA. These expressions are integrated into a PSRR-aware lookup table (LUT)-based $g_{m} / I_{d}$ sizing flow, applicable across various LDO topologies and technology nodes. A design example in a 180-nm CMOS process demonstrates the approach, achieving a DC-PSRR of −58.5 dB and a worst-case PSRR of −38.6 dB across a wide frequency range of DC to $\mathbf{1 ~ G H z}$.

title: Low Power 2nm Ultra-Low Voltage SRAM Operating at 0.35V with Enhanced Read/Write Circuitry
authors: K M Aishwarya, Sanatkumar Upadhye, Manish Trivedi, Jaswinder Sidhu, Ramesh Halli
publish: 2025 IEEE 6th International Women in Technology Conference (WINTECHCON)
abstract: The integration of Neural Processing Units (NPUs) is key to accelerate the artificial intelligence workloads, facilitating real-time execution of complex neural network models while significantly reducing power. The high parallelism and memory bandwidth demands makes the on-chip SRAM as indispensable. System-level power efficiency can be improved by operating at lower voltages. However, the approach is limited by the lowest voltage at which the SRAM design can sustain reliable read and write operations. Consequently, the system’s minimum operating voltage (Vmin) becomes bounded, restricting the extent of power efficiency gains achievable through voltage scaling. To address this limitation, the paper presents an SRAM design capable of operating at an ultra-low voltage of 0.35V by incorporating read and write margin improvement circuitry. The proposed design has been successfully implemented in a 2nm single-port SRAM instance, achieving 100% silicon yield while meeting the targeted power and performance metrics.

title: Iterative Decision-Making in Reinforcement Learning for Ride-Sharing Optimization: A Joint Approach to Dispatching and Rebalancing
authors: Hyun Su Park, DongHwa Shin, Shin Hyoung Park, Shin-Hyung Cho
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: This study proposes a reinforcement learning (RL)based decision-making framework to optimize operations in ridesharing (RS) systems. The framework simultaneously addresses two core challenges: passenger-vehicle dispatching and idle vehicle rebalancing under dynamic and spatially imbalanced demand conditions. A Deep Q-Network (DQN) is employed within a centralized architecture, complemented by an iterative Q-valuebased action selection strategy that accounts for inter-vehicle dependencies. To enhance computational efficiency, the framework incorporates feasibility-based action masking. The reward function integrates operational efficiency, service quality, rebalancing effectiveness, and environmental sustainability to support multi-objective learning. Experimental results across four spatial demand scenarios demonstrate that the proposed approach improves system throughput and passenger experience compared to baseline methods. Additional analysis confirms the effectiveness of rebalancing in reducing detour time and increasing request acceptance rates. The framework also shows robust learning convergence and policy generalization in both single- and multivehicle settings, highlighting its scalability and practical applicability to real-world, large-scale RS environments.

title: Joint Noise Whitening and Oversampled MRC Detection for W-OTFS Systems in Gamma-Gamma Turbulent Channels
authors: Tiantian Zhang, Minghua Cao, Panpan Fan, Shiyuan Zhang, Chunfang Chen, Yue Zhang
publish: 2025 13th International Conference on Information and Communication Networks (ICICN)
abstract: We propose a wavelet-based orthogonal time-frequency space (W-OTFS) modulation scheme by integrating wavelet transform (WT) into optical OTFS modulation. This scheme employs oversampled maximum ratio combining (MRC) for signal detection over Gamma-Gamma turbulence channels, enhancing the robustness of communication in such turbulent environments. A comprehensive theoretical analysis of the performance of oversampled MRC in the OTFS domain is provided. Simulations are carried out to determine suitable wavelet bases, delay-Doppler parameters, and the oversampling factor G for improving detection accuracy. Additionally, the impact of channel noise whitening on the bit error rate (BER) is evaluated, showing significant enhancements in BER performance. Finally, the proposed oversampled MRC detection scheme demonstrates superior performance compared to alternative algorithms under varying turbulence intensities, including strong turbulence conditions.

title: Recognition of Crack Formation Modes in Laser Thermal Splitting Using Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Yuri Nikityuk, Vladislav Prokhorenko, Dmitry Kovalenko, Andrey Sereda
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Information, Control, and Communication Technologies (ICCT)
abstract: This paper considers the problem of predicting crack behavior during laser thermal splitting of silicate glass, an important material used in the production of microelectronics and optics components. Given the high requirements for edge quality and the need for early detection of deviations, it is proposed to use computer vision and deep learning methods to automate the control. The proposed approach is based on the ResNet-50 convolutional neural network adapted to the task of analyzing video data in real time. Fine-tuning of the last layers of the network made it possible to achieve high accuracy in classifying crack development. The results demonstrate the promise of using ResNet for problems of monitoring laser thermal splitting of brittle non-metallic materials.

title: Grid Search Optimised Ensemble of Pretrained Models for Multi-Class Brain Tumour Classification Using MRI Images
authors: Md. Abu Shayid
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Brain tumours can lead to severe outcomes, impacting various bodily functions. Therefore, accurately identifying types of brain tumours at an early stage is crucial for initiating the correct medication procedure. Although CNN models are commonly applied in medical image disorder classification, they often suffer from overfitting during the training period with inadequately labelled and limited, varied datasets. Current research employs transfer learning and ensemble models to address these challenges. A review of existing studies reveals a gap in models and weight ratios intended for use with ensemble techniques. In this research, a framework is presented where multiple CNN models with various designs are trained using transfer learning technique for a combination of brain tumour MRI, Figshare, SARTAJ, and Br35H datasets. A grid search algorithm-based optimisation was utilised for obtaining the optimal influences to integrate six effective convolutional neural network models in the ensemble approach. Our proposed model shows 99.85% accuracy, indicating that our offered ensemble model overtakes the prevailing findings and provides healthcare professionals with improved assistance in decision-making via its precise classification capabilities.

title: Explainable Federated Cardiovascular Risk with Compact MLPs
authors: Amit Hasan Sikder, Md. Sohanur Rahman Hridoy, Md. Ashikur Arman, Md. Arshad Ali, Md. Abdulla Al Mamun
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Cardiovascular disease remains a leading cause of premature mortality worldwide, yet the clinical data needed to build accurate predictive models are rarely centralized due to privacy, regulatory, and organizational barriers. We present a privacy-preserving heart-disease prediction framework based on federated learning (FL) that trains a shared model while patient records never leave their originating sites. Using the publicly available UCI heart-disease dataset from Kaggle, we construct a reproducible pipeline with deterministic splits, non-IID client partitioning, and a unified preprocessing stack applied consistently across clients. A single Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) is trained under six server-side optimization strategies FedAvg, FedMomentum, FedAdam, FedYogi, FedProx, and SCAFFOLD. Model selection relies on a held-out validation split; decision thresholds are tuned via grid search, and final performance is reported using Accuracy (88.6%), Precision (88.6%), Recall (91.2%), F1 (89.9%) and Brier score (0.0973). We employ (SHapley Additive exPlanations) SHAP values to provide clinician-oriented explanations of feature contributions. The proposed framework demonstrates that FL can deliver practical, interpretable heart-disease risk modeling without compromising patient privacy.

title: Design and Development of Optical Sensor Emulator for Verification of on-Board HDA Algorithm of Chandrayaan-3
authors: Vikas Singh, Shivani Bhargav, Umang Bhatia, Shitisha Bajpai, Pramod Mishra, Tanna Jaimin Hasmukhray, Abhishek Kunal, Ritesh Kumar Sharma, B Saravana Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents the design and development of an Optical Sensor Emulator (OSE) intended for the verification of the Hazard Detection and Avoidance Processor used in the Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3 missions. The OSE is designed to transmit simulated lunar image data to the Data Processing Unit (DPU) via a designated interface, facilitating the testing and validation of the Hazard Detection and Avoidance (HDA)'s functionality within the DPU. The development of the OSE involved the use of VHDL based FPGA design, MATLAB for image pre-processing, LabVIEW for graphical user interface, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C}$</tex> based software module for data transfer over USB interface. The emulator was realized on a custom Kintex-7 FPGA-based mother board with LVDS, USB daughter cards and serves as part of a ground checkout unit. It interfaces with a host PC via USB and connects to the DPU through a dedicated LVDS interface.

title: Frame-Wise Interactive Strategy on Video Streams for Road Friction Estimation Under Kinematic Constraints
authors: Weihua Wang, Haonan Ding, Ziwei Wang, Guodong Yin
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: The failure to estimate the road friction coefficient in a timely manner is a major contributing factor to the high incidence of accidents in transportation systems. This paper proposes a reliable road friction estimation method for video streams and designs a frame-wise interactive strategy. The method applies kinematic constraints to image data, optimizing the ROI extraction for real-world driving perspectives, particularly with robustness to trajectory changes during steering. During the estimation phase, we design a multi-patch fusion decision network (MFDNet) for more reliable detection of road surface types. Additionally, considering that real vehicle platforms use video streams rather than ‘image-level’ data, we design a frame-wise interactive strategy to reduce data redundancy, balance computational resources on the vehicle platform, and mitigate the negative effects of camera defocus and motion blur on road friction estimation. We collected a dataset on closed roads near campus and conducted experiments with the entire algorithm pipeline on a real vehicle. The results show that the proposed method is robust and of practical application value, providing higher safety margins while optimizing the autonomous driving performance of intelligent vehicles.

title: Development of a Driving Posture Assessment System for Posture Standardization Using a Single in-Cabin Camera
authors: Shiyan Cui, Hiroaki Hayashi, Mitsuhiro Kamezaki
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Poor driving posture can impair musculoskeletal health and reduce situational awareness by narrowing the field of view. Although previous research has defined standard driving positions through ergonomic investigations, few studies have used non-contact sensors for real-time monitoring. To address this issue, we propose a driver posture assessment system using a single incabin camera. Our method integrates YOLOX for driver detection and HRNet for skeletal keypoint estimation, combined with the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for body and seat segmentation. From these outputs, key posture angles are calculated from the geometry of the detected keypoints and by fitting the driver's silhouette relative to the seat. Each approach exhibits strengths and weaknesses; therefore, a Kalman filter is applied to fuse the two types of angle measurements, resulting in more stable and accurate posture estimates. We evaluate the proposed system by comparing its output to posture labels annotated based on established ergonomic studies, and the results show that the fused approach closely aligns with human assessments. We confirm that this continuous monitoring system can assist drivers in maintaining proper posture during regular driving.

title: Data-Driven Model Updating and Load Identification for Rotor Blades Using Neural Networks
authors: Ye Wei, Mengwei Lu, Zhenguo Zhang, Zhongliang Xie, Shiming Zhang
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: Accurate identification of distributed loads on helicopter blades based on feasible sensor measurements plays a crucial role in enhancing overall flight performance and ensuring operational safety. In this study, a novel approach based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks is proposed to construct a surrogate model for distributed force reconstruction. Unlike conventional regularization methods, the proposed framework enables reliable recovery of time-varying spatial load distributions without excessive dependence on parameter tuning or steady-state assumptions. Moreover, as a prerequisite for load identification, the rotor blade model is accurately updated using a Deep Neural Network (DNN). The methodology is validated on the well-established XH-59A rotor blade model, demonstrating the effectiveness of neural networks in both model correction and distributed load identification.

title: Deep Learning and Probabilistic Clustering for Anomaly Detection in Nuclear Power Plants
authors: Jinxiao Yuan, Shuang Lin, Lin Guo, Sui Fan
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: Anomaly detection in nuclear power plants (NPPs) is critical for ensuring operational safety and preventing potential failures. Traditional monitoring methods usually involve waiting for anomalies to reach a threshold limit before triggering corresponding alarms, by which time an accident has typically already occurred. With artificial intelligence, however, we can handle high-dimensional data and capture complex nonlinear relationships, enabling us to detect anomalies much faster. This paper proposes a hybrid approach combining Stacked Autoencoders (SAEs) with Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) to improve anomaly detection performance. The SAEs is employed to extract hierarchical features, followed by a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) optimized through a combined scoring metric integrating Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), and silhouette coefficient for probabilistic clustering of latent features. A dual-threshold strategy based on reconstruction error and cluster distance is then applied to identify anomalies. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively detects anomalies in NPP, exhibiting higher robustness and accuracy compared to single-method approaches. The framework not only enhances early fault diagnosis but also provides interpretable insights into anomaly sources, contributing to safer and more efficient nuclear power plant management.

title: Integrity-Monitored Deep Reinforcement Learning for Safe and Robust Autonomous Navigation
authors: Yuanyuan Zhang, Yingying Wang, Baoshan Song, Weisong Wen
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Ensuring safe and robust autonomous navigation under uncertainty remains a fundamental challenge for deploying deep reinforcement learning (DRL) in real-world applications. This paper presents an integrity monitoring deep reinforcement learning (IM-DRL) framework that incorporates integrity monitoring (IM) principles inspired by global navigation satellite systems (GNSS). A novel integrity cost model (ICM) is introduced to quantify observation trustworthiness through probabilistic metrics, enabling adaptive safety constraints under sensor degradation and transient faults. IM-DRL combines these integrity metrics with model-based uncertainty estimation, allowing the agent to dynamically adjust safety thresholds in response to varying observation quality. To further enhance robustness, the framework injects systematically biased Gaussian noise into observations, simulating realistic sensor degradation and generating risk-critical scenarios without adversarial training. The resulting policy optimization remains computationally efficient while ensuring verifiable safety. Experimental results show that IM-DRL reduces the collision rate by up to 60.6 % compared to baseline methods, and exhibits strong generalization and adaptability in environments with severe observation disturbances, demonstrating its promise for safe and robust autonomous navigation.

title: Consensus-Aware AV Behavior: Trade-Offs Between Safety, Interaction, and Performance in Mixed Urban Traffic
authors: Mohammad Elayan, Wissam Kontar
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Transportation systems have long been shaped by complexity and heterogeneity, driven by the interdependency of agent actions and traffic outcomes. The deployment of automated vehicles (AVs) in such systems introduces a new challenge: achieving consensus across safety, interaction quality, and traffic performance. In this work, we position consensus as a fundamental property of the traffic system and aim to quantify it. We use high-resolution trajectory data from the Third Generation Simulation (TGSIM) dataset to empirically analyze AV and human-driven vehicle (HDV) behavior at a signalized urban intersection and around vulnerable road users (VRUs). Key metrics, including Time-to-Collision (TTC), Post-Encroachment Time (PET), deceleration patterns, headways, and string stability, are evaluated across the three performance dimensions. Results show that full consensus across safety, interaction, and performance is rare, with only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 6 6 \%}$</tex> of AV-VRU interaction frames meeting all three conditions. These findings highlight the need for AV models that explicitly balance multi-dimensional performance in mixed-traffic environments. Full reproducibility is supported via our open-source codebase on GitHub.

title: Performance Evaluation of Galileo HAS SIS and IDD Channels During the Initial Service Phase
authors: Paolo Zoccarato, Antoine Grenier, Ciro Gioia, Joaquim Fortuny Guash, Matteo Paonni
publish: IEEE Journal of Indoor and Seamless Positioning and Navigation
abstract: Many industrial and scientific applications rely on high accuracy positioning. With the advancements of modern Global Navigation Satellite Systems constellations and the release of new services, access to a real time high-accuracy positioning anywhere in the world is a reality. To enable real-time Precise Point Positioning, Galileo High Accuracy Service (HAS) offers corrections without needing any additional infrastructures on the user side. In this paper, we compare the user positioning perfor mance obtained with HAS corrections received via Internet Data Dissemination and Signal-In-Space using the software JRC User Navigation Engine (JUNE), which implement the Galileo HAS reference user algorithm developed at the Joint Research Centre (JRC). The comparison is carried out using stations of the International GNSS Service located throughout the world, using data acquired since the start of the initial service phase (January 2023). The goal of the paper is to evaluate the achievable performance using the different HAS corrections, by reviewing the performance evolution of HAS since the beginning of the initial service phase, and determining the impact on the user performances.

title: A Visual Approach for Estimating Plant Growth During the Life Cycle of a Vineyard
authors: A. Bono, G. Vivaldi, C. Guaragnella, T. D’Orazio
abstract: In recent years, the development of automated methods for phenotypic assessments of observable plant traits is gaining interest because they can provide advantages over standard ones. This study presents a novel methodology to estimate the canopy volume of grapevine plants during their growth cycle using a low-cost red, green, blue and depth (RGB-D) sensor mounted on a ground-based platform. The aim is to address the need for cost-effective and easy-to-use systems that can operate under variable environmental conditions without requiring highly specific data acquisition constraints and advanced technical skills. Using a Microsoft Azure Kinect RGB-D camera, detailed 3-D images of the plants are captured. The iterative closest point (ICP) algorithm is then applied to reconstruct a full view of the plants useful to estimate the canopy volumes. The effectiveness of this approach is validated by comparing the volumetric estimates with the leaf area index (LAI) measures obtained with traditional agronomic techniques during significant phenological periods of the plants’ lifecycle. The results demonstrate a correlation between the two approaches, indicating the reliability of the proposed method and highlighting advantages in terms of precision. These outcomes demonstrate the potential of automated vineyard monitoring systems, providing reliable data to assess plant growth conditions.

title: Precision Balancing of Hemispherical Resonator Gyros via Vibration-Excitation-Based Unbalanced Mass Identification
authors: Ning Wang, Zhennan Wei, Guoxing Yi, Changhong Wang
abstract: The quality factor and its circumferential non-uniformity are critical parameters influencing the performance of Coriolis Vibratory Gyroscopes (CVGs), core components of inertial navigation systems. This study investigates the mechanism by which unbalanced mass induces anchor loss, degrading both quality factor and its non-uniformity in Hemispherical Resonator Gyros (HRGs), a high-precision CVG subclass. A vibration-excitation-based signal processing method is proposed to identify unbalanced mass, leveraging dynamic response analysis of HRG mechanical systems under 3-dimensional vibration excitation. Microgram-level mass balancing is achieved via ion beam sputtering, with theoretical and experimental validations confirming the method’s effectiveness. A dedicated unbalanced mass identification and balancing system is developed, and systematic balancing experiments are conducted. Results demonstrate a 97.3% reduction in quality factor non-uniformity (from 17.57% to 0.48%) post-balancing, with HRG performance enhancement validated through resonant stability tests. This study establishes a theoretical and technical foundation for advancing precision CVG manufacturing, contributing to the improvement of inertial navigation system reliability.

title: Compact Four-Port MIMO Antenna with Circular Polarization for Implanted Biomedical Devices
authors: Jiang-li Shang, Hai-Yang Hu, Yun-Hui Peng, Ying Yu, Li-Jie Xu
abstract: A four-port implanted multiple-input-multiple-output (MIMO) antenna with high-data-rate is proposed for IoT-enabled wireless communications, operating at the 2.45 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) band. The proposed antenna achieves circular polarization characteristics with a very compact configuration, which tends to receive or transmit signals between external devices. Additionally, the channel capacity of this antenna is as high as 19 bps/Hz for implantable devices used in telemedicine. By introducing an orthogonal rectangular-slot on both the radiation patch and the ground, the isolation level can reach up to 31 dB between any two elements. Meanwhile, a compact configuration of π × 4.5<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> × 0.13 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> is achieved, and two right-handed circularly polarized (RHCP) and two left-handed circularly polarized (LHCP) are formed by loading slots and short-pins. The simulation results show that a bandwidth from 2.32 GHz to 2.66 GHz is achieved with S11 below −10 dB, and the axial ratio bandwidth of four elements reaches 17.6%, 19.6%, 16.2%, and 13.3% below 3 dB, respectively. Finally, the measurement bandwidth of 2.3–2.6 GHz and the simulation bandwidth both cover the required operating frequency band well.

title: Motion Adaptation for Exoskeletons Across Users and Tasks Via Meta-Learning_supp1-3673986.mp4
authors: Long Cheng
abstract: Wearable exoskeletons can augment human strength and reduce muscle fatigue during specific tasks. However, developing personalized and task-generalizable assistance algorithms remains a critical challenge. To address this, a meta-imitation learning approach is proposed. This approach leverages a task-specific neural network to predict human elbow joint movements, enabling effective assistance while enhancing generalization to new scenarios. To accelerate data collection, full-body keypoint motions are extracted from publicly available RGB video and motion-capture datasets across multiple tasks, and subsequently retargeted in simulation. Elbow flexion trajectories generated in simulation are then used to train the task-specific neural network within the model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework, which allows the network to rapidly adapt to novel tasks and unseen users with only a few gradient updates. The adapted network outputs personalized references tracked by a gravity-compensated PD controller to ensure stable assistance. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared with standard imitation-learning and dynamic movement primitive (DMP)–based methods, the proposed framework reduces muscle activation for new users performing untrained tasks. These findings suggest that the proposed framework effectively improves task generalization and user adaptability for wearable exoskeleton systems.

title: OKG-LLM: Aligning Ocean Knowledge Graph with Observation Data via LLMs for Global Sea Surface Temperature Prediction
authors: Hanchen Yang, Jiaqi Wang, Jiannong Cao, Wengen Li, Jialun Zheng, Yangning Li, Chunyu Miao, Jihong Guan, Shuigeng Zhou, Philip S. Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Sea surface temperature (SST) prediction is a critical task in ocean science, supporting various applications, such as weather forecasting, fisheries management, and storm tracking. While existing data-driven methods have demonstrated significant success, they often neglect to leverage the rich domain knowledge accumulated over the past decades, limiting further advancements in prediction accuracy. The recent emergence of large language models (LLMs) has highlighted the potential of integrating domain knowledge for downstream tasks. However, the application of LLMs to SST prediction remains under explored, primarily due to the challenge of integrating ocean domain knowledge and numerical data. To address this issue, we propose Ocean Knowledge Graph-enhanced LLM (OKG-LLM), a novel framework for global SST prediction. To the best of our knowledge, this work presents the first systematic effort to construct an Ocean Knowledge Graph (OKG) specifically designed to represent diverse ocean knowledge for SST prediction. We then develop a graph embedding network to learn the comprehensive semantic and structural knowledge within the OKG, capturing both the unique characteristics of individual sea regions and the complex correlations between them. Finally, we align and fuse the learned knowledge with fine-grained numerical SST data and leverage a pre-trained LLM to model SST patterns for accurate prediction. Extensive experiments on the real-world dataset demonstrate that OKG-LLM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, showcasing its effectiveness, robustness, and potential to advance SST prediction. The codes are available in the online repository.

title: A Physical Model-Guided Framework for Underwater Image Enhancement and Depth Estimation
authors: Dazhao Du, Lingyu Si, Fanjiang Xu, Jianwei Niu, Fuchun Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Due to the selective absorption and scattering of light by diverse aquatic media, underwater images usually suffer from various visual degradations. Existing underwater image enhancement (UIE) approaches that combine underwater physical imaging models with neural networks often fail to accurately estimate imaging model parameters such as scene depth and veiling light, resulting in poor performance in certain scenarios. To address this issue, we propose a physical model-guided framework for jointly training a Deep Degradation Model (DDM) with any advanced UIE model. DDM includes three well-designed sub-networks to accurately estimate various imaging parameters: a veiling light estimation sub-network, a factors estimation sub-network, and a depth estimation sub-network. Based on the estimated parameters and the underwater physical imaging model, we impose physical constraints on the enhancement process by modeling the relationship between underwater images and desired clean images, i.e., outputs of the UIE model. Moreover, while our framework is compatible with any UIE model, we design a simple yet effective fully convolutional UIE model, termed UIEConv. UIEConv utilizes both global and local features through a dual-branch structure. UIEConv trained within our framework achieves remarkable enhancement results across diverse underwater scenes. Furthermore, as a byproduct of UIE, the trained depth estimation sub-network enables accurate underwater scene depth estimation. Extensive experiments conducted in various real underwater imaging scenarios, including deep-sea environments with artificial light sources, validate the effectiveness of our framework and the UIEConv model. Code is available at https://github.com/ddz16/UWEnhancer.

title: Graph-Augmented MAPPO for Dynamic Task Offloading in Edge Networks
authors: Shiyi Wang, Fei Ding, En Tong, Ye Wang, Aiguo Song
abstract: In multi-access edge computing (MEC) environments, optimizing task offloading and communication-aware resource allocation is challenging due to dynamic topologies, fluctuating channels, and heterogeneous link capacities. This letter proposes a Graph-Augmented Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (GA-MAPPO) framework under a centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE) paradigm. A Master Agent cooperative architecture integrates a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to model dynamic communication dependencies and bandwidth variations among edge nodes. The hybrid action space enables joint optimization of task offloading and transmission resource allocation. Experiments based on mobility and workload traces show that GA-MAPPO improves task completion rate and reduces latency compared with representative baselines, demonstrating its adaptability in dynamic MEC and Internet of Vehicles (IoV) scenarios.

title: Heterogeneous Multimodal Multilabel Behavioral Context Recognition Using Wearable Sensors and Mobile Devices
authors: Haodong Liu, Ye Zhang, Zhidan Liu, Hongyuan Zhu, Changhong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Accurate behavioral context recognition (BCR) is essential for advancing social intelligence in smart home settings and social companion pairing. With advancements in mobile computing technologies, wearable sensors, and mobile devices have become crucial for multimodal data collection to interpret behavioral context. However, existing methods may overlook the multilabel attribute of behavioral context, limiting their ability to model modality-to-label and label-to-label dependencies. Moreover, the unique label heterogeneity in BCR, arising from category-specific differences among labels, is often underestimated. To overcome these challenges, we propose a heterogeneous multimodal multilabel recognition method to capture the relationships between modalities and labels, while taking the label heterogeneity into account. Using specialized heterogeneous decoders with self-adaptive attention and a heterogeneous graph attention network based on the dual-level attention mechanism, our method captures complex dependencies among labels and modalities. Experiments on two benchmark datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our approach, with ablation studies validating the contributions of each component.

title: PM-fiber-based Lyot filter for suppressing gain narrowing in high-power femtosecond fiber laser amplification systems
authors: Kun Chen, Zhijie Shi, Qi Xu, Zhou Li, Leyan Yang, Xinyi Yan, Jing Li, Jinlong Zheng, Mingtao Peng, Jiahui Peng
abstract: High-peak-power Yb-doped fiber femtosecond lasers are powerful tools widely used in scientific and industrial fields. To increase femtosecond pulse energy, the chirped-pulse amplification technique is commonly employed to suppress nonlinear effects and prevent fiber damage by temporally stretching pulses before high-gain amplifiers. However, the gain-narrowing effect severely limits the amplified spectral bandwidth and consequently constrains the achievable compressed pulse duration. In this work, we demonstrate a compact polarization-maintaining (PM)-fiber-based Lyot filter that enables flexible spectral pre-shaping to overcome this challenge. Utilizing the birefringence of PM fibers, precise control of the spectral modulation period is achieved through simple fiber-length selection, while continuous tuning of the modulation depth and spectral shift is realized via wave-plate rotations. Furthermore, the reflective configuration with PM-fiber pigtails minimizes insertion loss and device size, offering a cost-effective, environmentally stable, and high-damage-threshold design. Experimentally, the amplified spectral width increases from 8.5 nm to 13.6 nm at a 1-MHz repetition rate, enabling a reduction in compressed pulse duration from 264 fs (without the filter) to 198 fs for 27-μJ pulses. Even at 100 kHz, the system still maintains a bandwidth exceeding 13 nm for 229-μJ pulses, confirming effective suppression of gain narrowing at high pulse energies. This robust Lyot filter paves the way toward practical all-PM-fiber femtosecond laser systems delivering high-energy, sub-200-fs pulses for advanced manufacturing and strong-field physics.

title: Tuning the Remanent Magnetic State in Square Ferromagnetic Nanorings
authors: C. P. Costa, Ana L. Dantas, A. S. Carriço
abstract: We present a micromagnetic study of remanent magnetic states in square Fe and permalloy nanorings, emphasizing the roles of field history and geometric confinement. Remanent configurations are obtained by saturating the rings under different field orientations and reducing the field to zero. The results reveal strong path dependence: diagonal and perpendicular saturation stabilize onion-like and vortex states, respectively, whereas edge-field saturation induces transitions among buckle-like, in-plane vortex, and fully three-dimensional vortex states. Phase diagrams show that these transitions are governed primarily by magnetostatic interactions, with stronger effects in Fe due to its higher saturation magnetization. These findings provide a strategy for deterministic remanent-state selection in square nanorings, relevant to multi-state magnetic and spintronic applications.

title: Machine learning-based co-design of advanced flipchip packages: co-optimization of package warpage, die stress, solder joint reliability, thermal management, and signal integrity
authors: Mohammad Rafiee, Maryam Hasheminasab, Farough Agin, Farah El Siss
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: Advanced flip-chip packages must satisfy thermal limits, chip–package interaction constraints, solder-joint reliability, and increasingly tight signal-integrity (SI) margins. These requirements are strongly coupled. A design change that improves one metric can degrade another. Prior studies often optimize thermal or thermo-mechanical metrics in isolation, or treat SI separately from package reliability. A unified workflow for concurrent thermal–mechanical–electrical screening at the package-architecture stage is still limited. This paper presents an ML-assisted co-design workflow for advanced flip-chip packages. The framework combines thermo-mechanical FEA, a steady-state thermal model, and full-wave EM analysis. Six objectives are considered jointly: junction-to-ambient thermal resistance (ΘJA), package warpage (Wpkg), maximum principal stress in the ELK/BEOL region (σELK), viscoplastic work-based fatigue indicators for first-level bumps and second-level BGAs (ΔWbump, ΔWBGA), and a differential insertion-loss metric (ILDD21, derived from SDD21). A Latin Hypercube Sampling spans key material, geometry, and thermal-stack parameters. SI is evaluated using discrete, manufacturable substrate-core options anchored by EM simulations. Random Forest and XGBoost surrogates are trained (5-fold cross-validation) for the thermo-mechanical and thermal objectives and embedded in NSGA-II to generate Pareto-optimal trade-offs. The Net Flow Method is then used to rank Pareto candidates and identify a transparent “champion” design, which is re-simulated as a physics-based check. The workflow enables rapid and explainable exploration of coupled trade-offs. It also provides a practical screening method for early-stage flip-chip package architecture selection under mixed thermal, reliability, and SI constraints.

title: President's Message
authors: Tom Braxton
abstract: Thank you. I enter 2026 as President of the IEEE EMC Society, and I'm genuinely honored to be here. Specific thanks go to Immediate Past President John LaSalle and his predecessor Vignesh Rajamani for their patient support as I followed behind them last year as President-Elect.

title: Structural Property of the Optimal Entanglement Policy for Quantum Network Switch: An MDP Approach_supp1-3670158.pdf
authors: Bin Luo
abstract: A quantum switch is one of the most fundamental network elements for connecting different quantum devices. In this paper, we explore the “optimal entanglement policy” of a quantum switch under a scenario where the stored and entangled qubits in the quantum switch may undergo a decoherence process. Finding an optimal entanglement policy is important as it enables a quantum switch to make a judicious basis measurement based on the number of existing link-level entanglements to maximize the “weighted throughput”. We use a Markov decision process framework to model the dynamics of the quantum switch, and theoretically reveal a threshold-based relationship between bipartite and tripartite policies under a very general class of weight functions with a weight parameter β. We further extend our analysis to uncover a threshold-based structure of optimal policies. In particular, the optimal policy for the quantum switch is a bipartite or tripartite policy when β is below a threshold β∗B or above a threshold β∗T, respectively. When β∗B < β < β∗T, the optimal policy becomes a “threshold-based and state-dependent entanglement policy”. We also extend the work to allow a mixture of bipartite and tripartite policies by introducing a state-dependent mixed action. We theoretically show threshold-related relationships between mixed and bipartite/tripartite policies. Moreover, we extend this analysis to study optimal policies and characterize the threshold-based structure when the mixed action is considered. We carry out extensive numerical experiments to confirm that the optimal entanglement policy has such structural property when the mixed action is considered or not.

title: Design and Vision-Based Control of a Compact Magnetically Actuated Soft-Tethered Colonoscope for Automatic Manipulation With Safety Constraints
authors: Yehui Li, Yichong Sun, Weibing Li, Ruoyu Xu, Wing Yin Ng, Philip Wai Yan Chiu, Zheng Li
abstract: Colorectal cancer remains a mortal disease worldwide. Robotic colonoscopes have been extensively developed to conduct colon screening in a patient-friendly and surgeon-friendly manner. However, vision-based automatic colonoscope manipulation under safety constraints is still under-explored. This article presents a compact magnetically actuated soft-tethered (MAST) colonoscope with enhanced autonomy level and safety. With an internal permanent magnet (IPM) embedded inside the distal tip, the MAST colonoscope is actuated by a Halbach-array configured external permanent magnet (EPM) mounted on a robot arm. Various EPM–IPM configurations are analyzed to optimize the actuation capability of the system, with an interpolation-based colonoscope pose estimation method proposed. Kinematics modeling of the system is conducted, based on which a finite-time convergent weighted sliding mode controller is designed to enable vision-based motion control. To ensure safety, a quadratic programming (QP)-based scheme integrates physical limits and collision avoidance, solved by a predefined-time convergent and noise-tolerant adaptive zeroing neural network. Experimental validation demonstrates accurate colonoscope pose estimation under static and dynamic conditions. The proposed QP-based control scheme outperforms conventional methods in visual servoing, showing faster convergence and better noise rejection. The MAST colonoscope successfully performed semi/fully-automatic clinical tasks in a colon phantom under multiple safety constraints.

title: Affective Wearable Haptic Interventions: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Jueun Lee, Tobias Röddiger, Sarah Makarem, Till Riedel, Kai Kunze, Michael Beigl
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Interactive, Mobile, Wearable and Ubiquitous Technologies
abstract: None

title: Large Language Models for Combinatorial Optimization: A Systematic Review
authors: Francesca Da Ros, Michael Soprano, Luca Di Gaspero, Kevin Roitero
abstract: None

title: Ring-patterned control for hyperexponential consensus of multi-agent systems with increasing scales
authors: Xiaoli Luan, Nengneng Qing, Fei Liu
abstract: The ring-patterned control is studied for a class of multi-agent systems (MAS) under directed ring interactions with time-varying weights, further accommodating increasing scales and achieving hyperexponential consensus with the dynamic weighted average steady state. For both MAS of scalar-agent and multivariable-agent, ring-patterned control is proposed by encoding the corresponding matrices into respective time-varying polynomials of the time-invariant ring-patterned base matrix. With these encodings to design control parameters, the ability to accommodate MAS with increasing scales is guaranteed by the property that the ring-patterned base matrix restricts and encapsulates all the effects on eigenvalues of the scale variation. Because of this, the proposed control no longer requires prior knowledge of all interaction information at all open moments of scale variation, as is the case in existing literature. Additionally, the time-varying polynomials are designed without infinity control parameters to ensure hyperexponential consensus, a faster convergence than any exponential counterparts. Finally, the consensus steady state has been proven to be a dynamic weighted average.

title: Facilitating Efficient Driving and Intersection Flow Using Auxiliary Traffic Signals for Traditional and Automated Vehicles
authors: Md Abdus Samad Kamal, A. S. M. Bakibillah, Nakakura Kenta, Kou Yamada, Jun-ichi Imura
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: This paper presents an optimal pre-signal-based traffic signal framework that enhances intersection traffic flows while enabling efficient driving behavior, suitable for traditional and automated vehicles (AVs). Specifically, we propose the inclusion of an auxiliary traffic signal at an early stop line located before the standard one at the intersection, which allows the leading vehicle to enter the intersection immediately at a green light with a higher speed, reducing the start-up loss time by activating the auxiliary green light slightly earlier than the main conventional traffic signal. The relative positioning of the early stop line is optimized numerically for effectively redesigning the signaling system, considering relevant safety issues, constraints, and objectives. The vehicle control system employs model predictive control (MPC) to minimize costs associated with velocity and acceleration while maintaining a safe following distance. Through microscopic traffic simulations, the performance of traditional and automated vehicles is analyzed within this new signal system. The findings indicate a marked improvement in average speed, travel duration, fuel efficiency, and idling time compared to conventional intersection traffic management.

title: PAC-ORAN: Power Saving CPU Scheduling Approach for Scalable Mobile UEs in Open RAN
authors: Saish Urumkar, Byrav Ramamurthy, Somayeh Mohammady, Sachin Sharma
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Networks and Telecommunications Systems (ANTS)
abstract: Open RAN is a next-generation wireless network technology that promotes flexibility, cost efficiency, and interoperability through disaggregation and open interfaces. Energy efficiency remains a key challenge, especially in scalable deployments with many connected User Equipments (UEs), where dynamic power management at the gNodeB is critical. In this paper PAC-ORAN (Power-saving Adaptive CPU Scheduling for Open RAN) is proposed which is an advanced CPU scheduling algorithm evaluated with a large number of connected UEs to gNodeb. PAC-ORAN includes an advanced method using moving average adaptive threshold selection and dynamically adjusting CPU core states and frequency tuning based on real-time CPU metrics. The evaluation of PAC-ORAN focuses on two key objectives: (1) Sustainability Goals: PAC-ORAN reduces CPU power consumption by 5% to 30%, lowers RAM power usage by 2% to 20%, and brings down CPU temperature by 2 to 3 degree Celsius. (2) Performance Goals: PAC-ORAN maintains bounded throughput even during power-saving operations and balances CPU frequency usage. The key contribution of this paper lies in the proposed PAC-ORAN algorithm tested on large number of connected UEs along with key findings from the PAC-ORAN algorithms impact on CPU and RAM power utilization as well as its impact on CPU temperature and frequency usage.

title: Energy-Efficient Train Timetable Optimization With Energy Storage Devices
authors: Lingyu Song, Entai Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: With the continuous expansion of urban rail transit systems, operational energy consumption has become a major barrier to the industry's sustainable development. To address this issue, this study proposes a coordinated optimization model that integrates train timetable design with energy storage device management, aiming to minimize systemwide energy consumption by maximizing the utilization of regenerative braking energy. The model adopts a hybrid energy management strategy that combines both instant and delayed utilization, enabling more effective recovery and redistribution of braking energy. By jointly optimizing train schedules and storage device operations under various operational scenarios, the proposed approach fully leverages system flexibility to enhance energy efficiency. Numerical experiments demonstrate that this coordinated strategy significantly improves energy recovery and utilization. Besides, further case studies are conducted to analyze the impact of different numbers of scenarios and trips on the results.

title: Experimental Deployment of a Reinforcement Learning Automated Vehicle Controller Supervised by a Control Barrier Function
authors: George Gunter, Matt Bunting, Jonathan Sprinkle, Daniel Work
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: In this work a reinforcement-learning (RL) policy for controlling an automated vehicle (AV) is fielded in live experiments under supervision from a control barrier function (CBFs). This work focuses on using the CBF to deploy the RL policy in an environment different from its original training while maintaining safety. The CBF provides a formal safety filter to maintain a minimum time-gap while allowing the RL policy to control the AV when safety is not of concern. The RL controller is trained in an environment designed for freeway traffic control, but is deployed onto urban arterial roads. The primary contribution of this work is to show through deployment that the CBF can maintain safety, allowing the RL policy to be deployed to a new environment. We also show that overall metrics of performance from training such as reward and estimated traffic flow energy remain close to theoretical expectations without a supervisory CBF. From this we conclude that CBFs may be an effective tool for safely deploying RL policies into new testing environments.

title: Fine-Grained Health Monitoring of Optical Modules in Computing Clusters using Transformers
authors: Aiyue Wang, Ningchun Liu, Congqi Shen, Jun Zhu, Tao Zou
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: Health monitoring of optical modules in large-scale AI computing clusters faces critical challenges: the extreme scarcity of negative samples hindering model training, and coarse binary ("normal/faulty") classification preventing proactive maintenance by obscuring gradual degradation. To overcome these limitations, this paper presents a fine-grained health assessment framework with Transformer auto-encoders. It incorporates expert-guided anomaly detection, leveraging expert knowledge to annotate initial faults and training the auto-encoder solely on normal data, eliminating reliance on scarce fault samples. The Transformer captures long-term temporal dependencies in multivariate time-series, enabling a 6-level health system (L0-L5) based on reconstructed errors, covering from inactive to irreversible fault for proactive maintenance. To mitigate misjudgment risks, a two-level verification mechanism calibrates samples initially classified into high-severity levels (L4-L5) using physical parameter thresholds. Experiments on 16,335,000 samples (0.00015% faults) validate its effectiveness in identifying degradation, supporting proactive maintenance.

title: A Condition-based Predictive Maintenance Framework: A Case Study of Railway Point Machines
authors: Qi Jiang, Wei Shangguan, Linguo Chai, Junjie Chen
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: In high-speed railway systems, the operational reliability of railway point machines is critical to ensuring train safety and punctuality. This paper proposes a predictive maintenance closed-loop framework that integrates multi-source sensing, intelligent diagnosis, and strategy-level decision-making, aiming to achieve continuous monitoring and proactive management of point machine states. The framework incorporates deep learning models for operational state recognition based on multimodal acoustic signals and employs a Dynamic Decision Network (DDN) to infer and predict the evolution of the health state. GeNIe software is used for visual modeling and probabilistic reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in state tracking, uncertainty quantification, and maintenance strategy recommendation, providing both a theoretical foundation and technical pathway for developing intelligent, predictive maintenance systems for railway infrastructure.

title: Tunable Terahertz Antenna Using Silicon-Based Reconfiguration for Biomedical Applications
authors: Vudattu Sri Deepika Aishwarya Lakshmi, Bappadittya Roy, K B S Sri Nagini
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents a compact and tunable terahertz (THz) antenna (TTA) featuring a octagonal radiating patch with a centrally embedded circular silicon region, designed to achieve dynamic frequency agility within the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.31-1.38 \text{THz}$</tex> range. The tunability is facilitated by modulating the conductivity of the silicon, which acts as a reconfigurable element, enabling real-time resonance adjustment without physical alteration of the structure. The antenna achieves a consistent gain of over 5.57 dBi across all tunable states, confirming its suitability for high-performance THz applications. A detailed analysis of co- and cross-polarization characteristics reveals a substantial polarization conversion capability, validating the efficiency of the design. The tuning mechanism is supported by a mathematical model based on the frequency-dependent permittivity of the silicon layer, linked to its variable conductivity. Owing to the non-ionizing nature of THz radiation and the antenna's compact footprint, the proposed design is highly applicable to biomedical applications, including early-stage cancer detection.

title: A Comparative Study of Design Approaches for Sub-THz Power Dividers Based on Gap Waveguide
authors: Abhay Dwivedi, Priyansha Kaurav, N. P. Pathak
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents wideband designs of Hshaped 1:4 power dividers optimized for the D-band, based on ridge gap waveguide (RGW) and ridge-groove gap waveguide (RG-GGW) technologies. Among the five proposed structures, three employ equal power division, while the remaining two implement unequal power distribution through controlled termination variations. The equal power dividers exhibit a maximum operational bandwidth of approximately 12 GHz, covering the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 1 3}-\mathbf{1 2 5} \mathbf{~ G H z}$</tex> range. The hybrid RG-GGW unequal divider offers a more selective response, with an effective bandwidth of about 3 GHz. The combination of wideband operation, low insertion loss, and geometrically tunable asymmetry underscores the suitability of these designs for high-performance D-band systems, including beamforming networks, slot array feeds, and compact sub-THz front ends.

title: Design of Elliptical Hexagonal Loop Polarizer for C/X Band Applications
authors: Emmadisetty Seshadri Sai Mohan, Ghanta Mahesh, Boya Umamaheshwar, Saranya Madheswaran, Rajeshkumar Venkatesan
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents a dual-band reflective polarizer operating in stop band between 4-14 GHz employing elliptical hexagonal loops. The proposed structure is composed of two concentric elliptical hexagonal loops nested into one another and implemented on FR4 substrate. The presented FSS has superior stopband transmission properties, with extensive analysis of reflection coefficient, bandwidth, and absorption behavior. Besides offering dual-band filtering, the model also supports linear-to-circular polarization (LP-to-CP) conversion, which improves signal stability and polarization efficiency.
