Recently proposed wire macro basis functions (MBFs) are used instead of standard basis functions, for representing the shield current in the BI part, with much reduced degrees of freedom. The results demonstrate that for the simple test cases considered, the MBFs yield just as accurate results as the standard basis functions, while reducing the required memory dramatically. This is a promising result, as such computational efficiency improvements are important toward full-wave analysis of challenging real-world cable coupling scenarios.

title: Self-Spurious Coupling Compensation Technique for Coaxial Cavity Resonator Filter
authors: Dinesh Kumar Singh, Dharmendra Singh, Jignesh S. Bhatt
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents self-spurious coupling compensation technique for coax cavity resonator based filters. A study has been performed for existing spurious coupling identification and suitable mitigation method has been suggested. This method results in improved transmission and group delay response from the filter. Compensation of spurious coupling has been achieved with simpler implementation of coupling structure. The suggested filter modification results in reduced filter fabrication complexity and cost. The simpler implementation of the suggested changes in filter modelling results in shorter lead time and faster tuning of the actual filter hardware. The suggested method has been implemented for the realization of 10-2-2 cascaded quadruplet filter. The proposed filter was fabricated and the simulation results have been validated through measurements.

title: Improving the Performance of DoA Estimation with Novel Regression-Based Deep Learning Models
authors: Anjali K.S., Manoj Kumar Panda
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Networks and Telecommunications Systems (ANTS)
abstract: Direction of Arrival (DoA) estimation is a widely researched topic with important applications. Traditional DoA estimation techniques fail to perform well under high levels of noise. Thus, deep learning (DL) based methods have recently gained traction. The classification-based DL approaches can provide accurate estimation, but at the expense of high computational costs. The regression-based DL models are promising in that they can provide good accuracy with lower computational costs. In this paper, we propose four new regression-based DL models for DoA estimation that are obtained by modifying an existing regression-based baseline model that is both accurate and computationally efficient. The major modification made to the baseline model is the introduction of a convolutional neural network (CNN) based feature extraction module, which, together with the appropriate choice of the type and size of the input (covariance matrix) and internal handling of complex number calculations inside the DL model, can provide up to ≈45-55% improvement in accuracy and a factor of ≈1.8 reduction in computation time depending on the level of noise and the type of source signal waveform used for DoA estimation.

title: Modeling and Optimization of Traffic Evacuation Using Optimal Control Model
authors: Yanglan Wang, Kenichi Soga
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Efficient traffic flow optimization is vital for effective traffic management, especially during emergencies such as evacuations, which require the rapid and safe movement of large numbers of vehicles under intense time constraints. This study presents an optimal control model designed to regulate traffic input over time in rural road networks, aiming to minimize total travel time and ensure the complete evacuation of at-risk vehicles. The model integrates link-specific dynamic equations, variable constraints, and the flow-density relationship. A real-world case study of an evacuation in Inverness, California, is used to demonstrate the model's applicability. Sensitivity analysis illustrates the impact of the weighting parameters, and a microscopic simulation validates that similar inputs in both microscopic and macroscopic simulations produce consistent output patterns. Results show that the optimal control model substantially reduces overall travel time compared to phased evacuation strategy.

title: Time Modulation-Based Enhanced Sum-Difference Monopulse Beamforming Pattern Generation Using SPDT Harmonic Switching Networks
authors: Avishek Chakraborty, Durbadal Mandal, Ambar Bajpai
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This article aims to propose an alternate method of generating power-efficient sum-difference monopulse pattern using time-modulated antenna arrays. Time modulated antenna arrays have an added advantage of harmonics generation which can be controlled for simultaneous multipattern communication systems with suitable beamforming switching networks. In this work, a phase centered-based movement of predefined subarray cluster is applied in the proposed Beamforming Network (BFN) to produce simultaneous sum and difference patterns beneficial for monopulse radars. To enhance the efficiency of the switching network, a Single-Pole-Double-Throw (SPDT) based sequence is proposed. A 2N element symmetrically arranged linear antenna array is considered, and a suitable subarray cluster-controlled SPDT time scheme is implemented. The detailed comparison has been drawn between the phase-centered motion-based simple ON-OFF time scheme and the proposed enhanced SPDT-based harmonic BFN to show a superior monopulse performance.

title: A Packaged 89 GHz LNA MMIC for High-Sensitivity Atmospheric Sounding
authors: Piyush Sinha, Mukeshkumar K. Patel, Ch. V. N. Rao
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents the design, simulation, packaging, and measurement of an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 9 ~ G H z}$</tex> low noise amplifier (LNA) monolithic microwave integrated circuit (MMIC) developed using the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0 ~ n m}$</tex> metamorphic high electron mobility transistor (mHEMT) D007IH process from OMMIC foundry, France. While previous studies primarily report W-band LNAs at the bare-die level, this work emphasises a fully packaged LNA using WR-10 waveguide transitions and bondwire compensation networks, enabling practical deployment in atmospheric sounding systems. The LNA demonstrates a measured noise figure of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3. 6 ~ d B}$</tex> and a gain of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 3. 6 ~ d B}$</tex> over a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3}$</tex> GHz bandwidth. Co-simulated electromagnetic (EM) models, including transition and bondwire structures, show strong correlation with measurements, establishing this as a viable W-band packaged solution.

title: Clinically Interpretable Deep Learning for Genomic-Based Glioma Subtyping Using SHAP
authors: Fahima Hossain, Sharmin Akter, Era Aich, Rumi Akter, Ananna Datta
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: The most common and devastating type of the primary malignant tumor in the central nervous system (CNS) is glioma, which requires precise treatment planning and prognostic determination based on the accurate and rapid grading (Low-Grade: WHO II/III vs. High-Grade: WHO IV). Although Deep Neural Network (DNN) models, which combine multi-omics data (e.g., clinical and genomic features), are highly accurate in such classification, their black box characteristics are devastating to clinical trust and uptake. In this work, we propose a new framework of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) in order to bridge this important gap in glioma grading. We have created Comprehensive Deep Learning (CMDL) model to classify gliomas as Low-Grade (Grade 0) and High-Grade (Grade 1) based on clinical data and important somatic mutations (e.g., IDH1, TP53, PTEN). The decision making process of the model is clearly described based on the SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) approach. SHAP analysis is a quantitative approach to establishing how each data feature contributes to predicting the model both locally (patient-specific) and globally. Findings indicate that the model has a high predictive accuracy (e.g., up to ~90%), and is at the same time able to explain the biological validity of genomic markers in the determination of grades. The proposed XAI-enabled CMDL framework encourages the transparent, reliable, and clinically applicable application of AI to neuro-oncology, which is a major advance towards Precision Medicine.

title: An Interpretable State-Aware Variational Network with Dual Invertible Flows for Remaining Useful Life Prediction of Aero-Engines
authors: Ruoyao Tian, Biao Mei, Weidong Zhu
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction is a crucial task in Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) of aero-engines, aiming to improve reliability and reduce maintenance costs. However, existing deep learning methods overlook the differences in feature distributions between healthy and degraded states, assuming that they follow the same simple distribution, which limits model’s flexibility and interpretability. To address these challenges, we propose SADF-VNet, a State-Aware and Dual-Flow Variational Network for interpretable RUL prediction. Specifically, SADF-VNet separately models healthy and degraded states through private invertible flows, while maintaining structural consistency and probabilistic alignment via shared layers and a common Gaussian prior. In this model, a local-global feature extractor captures multi-scale patterns, and an adaptive fusion module integrates them based on inter-feature correlations. After reparameterization, a state classifier predicts each latent variable’s degradation probability and assigns it to a dual-flow transformation: a lightweight affine flow for healthy samples and a spline-based flow for degraded samples, introducing greater distributional complexity. Finally, the latent representations are decoded by a regressor for RUL estimation, and the latent variable’s distribution provide intuitive RUL assessment. Experimental results on the C-MAPSS dataset demonstrate that SADF-VNet achieves both high prediction accuracy and interpretability.

title: Resource Allocation in Multicarrier In-Band Full-Duplex Communication Systems with Successive Interference Cancellation
authors: M Venkata Srinu, Krishna Chaitanya A, Satya Kumar Vankayala, Seungil Yoon
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Networks and Telecommunications Systems (ANTS)
abstract: We consider a multi-carrier single-cell in-band full-duplex (IBFD) enabled base station (BS) and half duplex users. IBFD system introduces significant challenges, including self-interference and co-channel interference. This paper address the issue of co-channel interference (CCI) at the downlink (DL) users due to parallel uplink transmission over the same frequency resource. We derive condition on channel gains for feasibility of successive interference cancellation (SIC) at DL users to combat this CCI. Also, we propose resource allocation algorithms to reduce the CCI. We perform numerical simulations to compare the performance of our algorithms with existing Hungarian algorithm. The simulation results show that proposed approach improves sumrate performance while reducing computational complexity.

title: Metasurface-Based High-Resolution Field Probe
authors: Mauricio Hernandez, Mohammad Omid Bagheri, Omar M. Ramahi
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents a novel concept for a metasurface field detector probe. The probe is composed of a collection of electrically small components arranged in a twodimensional array, with only the central component acting as the field detector. This first-generation electromagnetic probe achieves a resolution that exceeds that of traditional electrically small probes and resonance-based probes of similar size to the wavelength. The metasurface probe is designed to operate across a broad frequency spectrum by adjusting specific parameters, allowing significant miniaturization of cell architecture. Since the device needs to collect electromagnetic energy from the transmitted wave, it can be modeled as an absorber. The design of the probe aims to achieve optimal alignment to facilitate the deep penetration of electromagnetic waves into the absorber structure. In particular, this probe will be matched to the freespace impedance, thereby significantly reducing the reflection of microwave energy.

title: A Wideband Singly Balanced Gilbert Cell Active Mixer in Microstrip for Drone Carried Surveillance Systems
authors: Shubham Kumar, Ananjan Basu
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: A novel approach to design, fabricate and test a Singly Balanced Gilbert Cell mixer for wideband application for incoming RF signal (0.5 to 6 GHz) over RO Rogers 4350 B 20 mil substrate has been undertaken. The mixture shall serve as the first mixing stage of a WB detector which can be implemented onboard UAVs for air surveillance and reconnaissance. This can be effectively used in state of art, low cost, light-weight swarm drones for transforming surveillance operations as compared to traditional high-end, conventional drone systems by offering significant operational, tactical and economical superiority. The design also incorporates passive components viz. a WB LO Balun (for 7 to 12.5 GHz LO), 4 section parallel coupled BPFs and 180 deg Hybrid designed at 6.5 GHz center frequency, to obtain an IF of 6.5 GHz. The mixer has demonstrated significant IF power output and Conversion Gains (CG) up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 3 ~ d B}$</tex> at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 5}$</tex> GHz and better than -8 dB across the RF band (considering cable losses). No stability concern has been encountered. Software worked upon in the project are ADS and CST.

title: AI-Driven Optimization of Microstrip Patch Antenna Arrays
authors: Shreyas Aradhya K, Samarth Babu Gotrale, Mahesh A, Ashutosh Kedar
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents an AI-driven approach for optimizing microstrip patch antennas using optimization algorithms Differential Evolution (DEA) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). A coax-fed patch operating at 5.65 GHz is extended to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \times 8$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times 2$</tex> arrays, culminating in an optimized <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8 \times 8$</tex> array. The process of interaction between algorithms in the Python environment and HFSS was automated. The design parameters were updated iteratively, and optimization is carried out further to get better performance. For a single-element coax-fed antenna, PSO achieved 44.42 dB return loss and 7.80 dBi gain, and DEA achieved 17.65 dB return loss and 8.11 dBi gain, outperforming traditional HFSS parametric sweeps while reducing optimization time by 50%. For the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times 2$</tex> array, PSO achieved 28.56 dB return loss and 12.99 dBi gain, and DEA achieved 25.85 dB return loss and 12.73 dBi gain with a 70% reduction in optimization time compared to HFSS parametric sweeps.

title: A FPGA Implementation of Secure Wireless Communication Based on Digital Chaotic System
authors: Jing Li, Yuanxiang Chen, Chaoyi Du, Shiyin Wang, Kai Sun, Jiawei Xin, Leijing Yang
publish: 2025 13th International Conference on Information and Communication Networks (ICICN)
abstract: To address the growing information security demands in wireless communications, this paper proposes a communication module based on a four-dimensional digital chaotic system. Implemented through an AD9361-ZYNQ platform, the module achieves wireless transceiver functionality with 16 Quadrature Amplitude Modulation(16-QAM) modulated secure transmission of textual and visual data, enabling accurate decryption at the receiving terminal. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the system possesses a key space of approximately 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">180</sup>, exhibiting robust resistance against brute-force attacks. Simulation results demonstrate that encrypted images exhibit superior performance in pixel distribution uniformity, spatial correlation elimination, and information randomness, indicating resistance to statistical analysis attacks. Hardware measurements reveal reliable transceiver operation with exceptional error correction capability, achieving a bit error rate (BER) on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-9</sup>, while unauthorized decryption attempts maintain BER of approximately 44%. These substantiate the practical viability of the proposed scheme for secure wireless communication applications.

title: Complexity-Optimized Vector-Switching Digital Predistortion Using Basis-Propagation Selection for RF Power Amplifiers
authors: Shipra, Thomas Eriksson
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: A novel digital predistortion (DPD) framework is developed by integrating the vector-switched (VS) modeling approach with the Basis-Propagation Selection (BAPS) model to enable highly efficient and accurate linearization of nonlinear radiofrequency (RF) power amplifiers (PAs). In the proposed VS-BAPS architecture, the input signal space is partitioned into multiple regions via clustering on input amplitude statistics, and within each region, an independent BAPS model is identified using a greedy basis construction algorithm. This structure exploits the adaptive local modeling capability of the VS model while incorporating the complexity-optimized basis generation of the BAPS approach. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the VS-BAPS model achieves superior linearization performance in terms of normalized mean square error (NMSE) and adjacent channel power ratio (ACPR), while significantly reducing computational complexity compared to conventional generalized memory polynomial (GMP), vector-switched GMP (VS-GMP), and standalone BAPS models.

title: A Modular Framework Approach Towards Ris-Assisted JSAC Using AoA Estimation
authors: S Dharshne, Adarsh Kumar Nayak
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper proposes a generic framework for Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS)-assisted Joint Sensing and Communication (JSAC) using angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimation and directional beam control. The framework enables real-time tracking of Radio Frequency (RF) tags, leveraging phase-based AoA estimation to dynamically steer RF beams through RIS codebook reconfiguration. It integrates an actuation layer for mechanical tracking and a control layer that maps estimated directions to pre-calibrated RIS states, achieving an average AoA accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 5^{\circ}$</tex> within a 10 m range. A visual codebook emulation layer aids analysis and debugging. The proposed architecture provides a modular and implementation-ready foundation for developing and evaluating RIS-assisted JSAC systems, demonstrating practical feasibility for intelligent, direction-aware wireless environments.

title: AI-Driven Analysis of Sleep Disorders in Bangladeshi University Students Using Clinical and Behavioral Insights
authors: Najma Akter Lopa, Sultan Mahamud Opu, Sourav Roy, Ifat Zahan Soma, Pankaj Bhowmik
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Sleep disorders are a rising problem among university students, affecting their mental and physical health and reducing their academic performance. In this work, we propose a comprehensive machine learning framework, enhanced with explainable artificial intelligence, to detect sleep disorders and analyze clinical and behavioral factors collected from several public universities in Bangladesh. We applied various cleaning and preprocessing methods, including feature skewness and kurtosis analysis, label encoding, manual mapping of categorical variables, feature correlation analysis using the Pearson correlation coefficient, and feature importance analysis with the Random Forest classifier. Outliers were managed using the Z-Score method. We trained and evaluated nine machine learning (ML) models to detect sleep disorders. The models included Logistic Regression (LR), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), Decision Tree, Random Forest (RF), AdaBoost, CatBoost, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, and Support Vector Machine (SVM). Among these, LR achieved the highest accuracy of 95.16%. We also performed cross-validation using a secondary dataset gathered from Kaggle. After optimizing LR with Bayesian hyperparameter tuning, accuracy reached 97.58% with an F1-Score of 97.21%. For explainability, we used SHAP to interpret predictions. Finally, we deployed our framework as an AI-driven decision support system (DSS) for sleep disorder diagnosis, with the aim of contributing to automation in clinical healthcare practices for university students.

title:
Design and Numerical Analysis of a Highly Sensitive SiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    /Al/As
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    S
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    /AlN SPR Sensor for SARS-CoV-2 Variant Detection
authors: Al Hadith Moon, Md. Ekramul Reza, Rahatuzzaman Fardin, Nazmus Sakib Saad
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Rapid and sensitive detection of viral biomarkers is crucial for modern diagnostic applications, particularly in the context of emerging variants such as SARS-CoV-2 Omicron (B.1.1.529). Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensing provides a powerful label-free platform, enabling real-time monitoring of molecular interactions with high precision. In this work, we propose a multilayer prism-based SPR biosensor designed for Omicron variant detection. The sensor structure comprises a SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> prism (1.4 µm), a 45 nm aluminum (Al) plasmonic layer, a 10 nm Arsenic Trisulfide (As<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>) dielectric spacer, a 5 nm aluminum nitride (AlN) layer. The sensor performance was analyzed using the Transfer Matrix Method (TMM) in MATLAB and validated through Finite Element Method (FEM) simulations in COMSOL Multiphysics. Both methods produced consistent reflectance spectra, confirming the reliability of the analytical model. The optimized configuration achieved a sensitivity of 550°/RIU, detection accuracy of 0.26 deg⁻<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>, a quality factor of 143.60 RIU⁻<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>, these results surpass several recently reported nanomaterial-assisted SPR biosensors, demonstrating the strong potential of the proposed design for next-generation point-of-care diagnostics.

title: A Comprehensive Review on Mitigating Partial Shading in Photovoltaic Systems
authors: Bishwajit Banik Pathik, Nahim Amin, Sharajin Jaima Rahman
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Performance and reliability of PV systems are facing major challenges in partial shading conditions (PSC). These drawbacks occur when the current mismatch and hot spot are brought about by an obstruction such as clouds, trees or buildings which shadow the module so that energy production is diminished and module damage may have occurred. This paper reviews different solutions to tackle PSC induced aging, such as array reassociation methods, improved MPPT algorithms or power electronics development. Layout reconfiguration techniques, such as Sudoku layout and Honeycomb layout, and dynamic switching have been demonstrated to achieve better tolerance to shading. Moreover, AI-supported MPPT algorithms such as PSO and GAs also present better global MPPT performances under partial shading. In addition, the implementation of advanced converter topologies has been important to ensure steady power generation under partial shading. The combination of these methods improves the PV system robustness, efficiency and life time against various shading situations. Subsequent studies need to concentrate on validation of the proposed techniques in a real and dynamic environment, hybrid MPPT approaches and combination of them with energy storage system for maximization of performance factors under diverse conditions.

title: Misalignment Mitigation in Low-Frequency Mammography Subtractive Imaging
authors: Farzan Mirshekari, Ghazaleh Tashtarian, Mauricio Hernandez, Hamid Akbari-Chelaresi, Omar M. Ramahi
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: Detecting breast cancer using electromagnetic waves has been an area of intense research in the past twenty years. One of the methods proposed recently calls for interrogating the right and left breasts. Once images of both are obtained, subtractive imaging is performed to determine the existence, if any, of anomalies between the two breasts that, in turn, could be related to a benign or cancerous tumor in one of the two breasts. This subtractive imaging requires perfect alignment with respect to the detection platform and the radiating element. This work presents a method to mitigate this possible misalignment.

title: Asymmetric-Response Bandpass Filter with Multiple Transmission Zeros Using Frequency-Variant Couplings
authors: Vinay B. Narayane, Girish Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: In this paper, a simple filter configuration is proposed to achieve asymmetric transmission response with higher roll-off on one side of the passband and second harmonic suppression. The proposed method involves realizing frequencyvariant couplings in two different forms which provide multiple transmission zeros. The concept is proven through simulation and measurement. The prototype filter designed at center frequency of 3 GHz and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$15-\text{dB}$</tex> return loss bandwidth of 53 MHz shows measured insertion loss of 1.28 dB with harmonic suppressed stopband up to 2.8 times the center frequency.

title: A New Dynamical System with Infinite Coexistence Attractor and Its Application in Secure Communication
authors: Wandong Xue, Xiaoying Song, Xianyu Hu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Information Communication and Software Engineering (ICICSE)
abstract: To improve the resilience of secure communication systems, a sine function is embedded into the Sprott C model, resulting in a new dynamical system with infinitely many coexisting attractors. First, theoretical analysis verifies the system's complex dynamical properties and rich state evolution patterns, laying the foundation for its application in information processing. Second, circuit simulation using the Multisim demonstrates the system's feasibility and stability at the hardware level. Finally, the system is combined with an image encryption algorithm and experimentally verified in a secure communication scenario. The results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively improves the security and anti-interference capabilities of image encryption. This research provides new insights and references for the application of complex dynamical systems in information security and confidential communications.

title: Enhancing Multi-class Skin Cancer Image Classification Using Self-supervised Vision Transformer Architecture
authors: Md. Mosabbir Hossain, Mohammod Abul Kashem
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Early and precise diagnosis of skin cancer is essential to proper and improved treatment and patient outcomes. Traditional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are known to be very effective in classifying skin lesions but they need large labelled datasets and have difficulty in capturing the global context data. This paper is an attempt to overcome these limitations by introducing a self-trained Vision Transformer (ViT-S/16) using the Distillation with NO labels (DINO) architecture to classify multi-class skin cancer. The pretrained DINO-ViT-S/16 model was fine-tuned using a highly imbalanced dataset, HAM10000, on which some lesion classes have only a number of samples, which poses a risk of overfitting. To overcome this, there was the application of a dynamic on-the-fly augmentation and image-weighting strategy apllied to mitigate it and stratified 5-fold cross-validation used for robust evaluation. Through experimental results, it is shown that the proposed model achieved an accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score of 95.86%, 95.30%, 95.44%, and 95.88% respectively, compared to the traditional CNN, pretrained CNN (ResNet50, EfficientNet, and Xception) and Vision Transformer baseline. Such results demonstrate the usefulness of self-supervised DINO Vision Transformers to learn powerful feature representations of dermatological images, which is a promising strategy in diagnosing skin cancer with high accuracy, in limited amounts of data, and with balance.

title: Exploring the Design and Implementation of Situational Digital Games in Elementary School Health Education Curriculum
authors: Yin-Yu Chou, Chun-Yen Huang, Shu-Han Lin, Chia-Wei Liu, Pei-You Lin, Ting-Yu Huang, Pin-An Chen
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 9th International Conference on Education and Multimedia Technology
abstract: None

title: Oscillating Eel Robots that Leverage Collisions for Controllable Motions
authors: Dapeng Wang, Steven Ceron
abstract: We introduce a platform of oscillating eel-like robots that exploits physical collisions to enable controllable collective behaviors. Each robot is actuated by a Kuramoto-style central pattern generator, and produces undulatory motion that passively synchronizes through collisions with neighboring robots and a surrounding rigid membrane. Instead of avoiding collisions, the system leverages robot-robot and robot-environment interactions to bias collective forces and torques. Using motor current as a surrogate for joint torque, we analyze how actuation parameters enable specific collective motions, and by tuning a small set of global central pattern generator parameters and selectively locking joints or robots, the collective can be programmed to exhibit clockwise and counterclockwise rotation, orbiting with adjustable curvature, and directed translation. We further demonstrate that the collective behaviors can be switched between on demand to enable trajectory following, obstacle avoidance, and object manipulation.

title: Sense4FL: Vehicular Crowdsensing Enhanced Federated Learning for Object Detection in Autonomous Driving
authors: Yanan Ma, Senkang Hu, Zhengru Fang, Yun Ji, Yiqin Deng, Yuguang Fang
abstract: To accommodate constantly changing road conditions, real-time vision model training is essential for autonomous driving (AD). Federated learning (FL) serves as a promising paradigm to enable autonomous vehicles to train models collaboratively with their onboard computing resources. However, existing vehicle selection schemes for FL all assume predetermined and location-independent vehicles' datasets, neglecting the fact that vehicles collect training data along their routes, thereby resulting in suboptimal vehicle selection. In this paper, we focus on the fundamental perception problem and propose Sense4FL, a vehicular crowdsensing-enhanced FL framework featuring trajectory-dependent vehicular training data collection to improve the object detection quality in AD for a region. To this end, we first derive the convergence bound of FL by considering the impact of both vehicles' uncertain trajectories and uploading probabilities, from which we discover that minimizing the training loss is equivalent to minimizing a weighted sum of local and global earth mover's distance (EMD) between vehicles' collected data distribution and global data distribution. Based on this observation, we formulate the trajectory-dependent vehicle selection and data collection problem for FL in AD. Given that the problem is NP-hard, we develop an efficient algorithm to find the solution with an approximation guarantee. Extensive simulation results have demonstrated the effectiveness of our approach in improving object detection performance compared with existing benchmarks.

title: Enhancing Cross-Scenario Generalization in Indoor Localization via Feature Disentanglement
authors: Zhendong Xu, Manyu Xue, Xuemei Xiong, Huizi Hu, Yi Liu, Jiankun Zhang, Hao Wang, and Yuan Shen
abstract: Deep learning based methods have been increasingly applied to wireless localization and sensing. However, many existing methods suffer from poor generalization, which limits their adaptability to unseen environments. Additionally, retraining these models demands a substantial amount of labeled data, which is both time-consuming and labor-intensive. To address these challenges, we propose GenLoc, a generalization framework that enables accurate mitigation of ranging and angle estimation errors in unseen environments via feature disentanglement, without the need for additional data collection and online training. The key lies in learning fine-grained domain-invariant representations that are first extracted by minimizing feature distribution discrepancies across various domains and then refined through domain-invariant and domain-specific feature decoupling. Extensive experiments were conducted on real-world datasets, covering five distinct scenarios, each with five different obstacles, and a thorough comparison with several existing approaches illustrates that GenLoc improves average distance and angle estimation accuracy by more than 22% and 28%, respectively, while others exhibit negligible improvements or significant degradation.

title: IoT UWB-Radar Array System with Two-Stage Multi-Task Deep Network for Bed Occupancy and Posture Surveillance via Spatial Echo Feature Map and Cross-Modal Visual Explainability
authors: Liwen Zha, Minxin Chen, Andy Yiu-Chau Tam, Duo Wai-Chi Wong, James Chung-Wai Cheung

title: Viewpoint: Next-Generation Sensor Technologies for Studying Mental Health
authors: Christina H. Liu, Yvonne M. Bennett
abstract: Sensors of all kinds continue to be further refined and developed for a wide range of applications, including in the biomedical domain. Sensors are routinely used for vital sign tracking and motion. However, more work is needed to further enhance research efforts in the study of mental health. This letter highlights this area of bioelectronics and biosensing to encourage researchers to improve capabilities in electrochemical sensing - a gap area, to enable synchronous monitoring of brain activity and behavior, leading to new insights into brain circuit function and improving closed-loop therapies.

title: Block-Wise Mixed-Order Continuity Constraints for Displacement Estimation in Transcranial Ultrasound Shear Wave Elastography
authors: Yiran Chen, Chaoyang Zhang, Hanbing Chu, Yichen Yan, Xiao Su, Yueyuan Wang, Liyuan Jiang, Meiling Liang, Hongmei Zhang, Mingxi Wan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Transcranial ultrasound shear wave elastography (TSWE) enables noninvasive assessment of brain tissue mechanical properties for diagnosing brain diseases. Accurate shear wave displacement estimation is critical for reliable evaluation of tissue mechanics. However, the thickness and heterogeneous structure of the skull induce strong attenuation and scattering of both ultrasound and shear waves. These effects degrade ultrasound tracking quality, weaken shear wave propagation in the brain, and introduce substantial noise, thereby reducing the accuracy of shear wave displacement estimation. To address this challenge, a block-wise mixed-order continuity constraints (BW-MOUL) method is proposed. This method incorporates first- and second-order regularization to enhance displacement accuracy by improving spatial smoothness and noise suppression, while employing a block-wise matrix strategy to improve computational efficiency. In numerical simulations, cranial phantom experiments, and in vivo goat brain experiments, BW-MOUL demonstrated superior displacement estimation performance, thereby enhancing the accuracy of shear wave speed (SWS) estimation and the reliability of viscoelasticity and fluidity assessment. In simulations, BW-MOUL achieved a normalized root-mean-square error (NRMSE) of 4.03% in displacement estimation relative to the ground truth. In phantom experiments, BW-MOUL improved the particle displacement signal-to-noise ratio (PDSNR) by 5.11 dB and 3.27 dB, and increased the SWS SNRt of the tumor region by 10.79 and 4.61, compared to normalized cross-correlation (NCC) and global ultrasound elastography (GLUE), respectively. In goat brain experiments, BW-MOUL yielded elasticity, viscosity, and fluidity values of 1452 Pa, 7.077 Pa·sα, and 0.9291, respectively, which are consistent with reported goat brain tissue properties. Moreover, the block-wise processing strategy achieved approximately a 66-fold speedup over the global method. Overall, the BW-MOUL method demonstrates strong potential in TSWE for noninvasive diagnosis of brain diseases.

title:
Carbon Nanotube Surface Engineering Through Supercritical CO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Pretreatment for High-Quality Gate Dielectric Deposition
authors: Zifan Li, Jiazheng Liu, Songquan Yang, Mingchao Yang, Chuanyu Han, Xin Li, Li Geng, Yubin Yuan, Weihua Liu
abstract: Atomic layer deposition (ALD) has emerged as an important technique for dielectric fabrication in single-walled carbon nanotube field-effect transistors (SWCNT FETs). Notably, intrinsic SWCNTs are chemically inert and lack sufficient nucleation sites for the ALD process, which limits both the minimum thickness and interface quality of the dielectric layer. In this work, a pretreatment of SWCNTs with supercritical CO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> (SCCO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>) fluids is introduced before ALD to improve the deposition quality. The SCCO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> pretreatment SWCNTs (SC-SWCNTs) demonstrate an increased number of nucleation sites on the surface for ALD dielectric fabrication, thereby improving the quality of the deposited gate dielectric layer and effectively reducing the interface state density. The experimental results reveal that the SCCO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-pretreatment SWCNT FET (SC-SWCNT FET) has an enhanced breakdown voltage of the dielectric layer and a reduction in interface state density, accompanied by a two-order-of-magnitude enhancement in the current <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>/<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small> ratio. The SCCO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> treatment demonstrates superior dielectric-SWCNTs interface modification capabilities, establishing a viable pathway for developing high-performance SWCNT FETs for next-generation nanoelectronics applications.

title: Integrating Three-class Classification Strategy and Cross-domain Feature Sets for UWB Non-Line-of-Sight Identification and Error Correction
authors: Xin Sui, Zhengjun Gong, Changqiang Wang, Hongchao Zhao, Jiapeng Song, Li Luo, Bangwen Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Aiming at the problem that it is difficult to accurately identify Ultra-Wide-Band (UWB) Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) signals and there exists ranging deviation, this paper proposes a UWB NLOS identification and error correction method that integrates a three-class classification strategy and a cross-domain feature set. Firstly, the UWB signal propagation states are divided into Line-of-Sight (LOS), NLOS, and High Confidence Non-Line-of-Sight (HCNLOS), improving the positioning accuracy and efficiency in different occlusion scenarios. A cross-domain feature set is then constructed by combining features from the signal and spatial domains, and a three-class classification model is built using Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) to accurately identify the UWB signal states. To further enhance the accuracy of the model, a hybrid algorithm based on Differential Evolution Adaptive (DEA) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is constructed to optimize the hyper-parameters of LightGBM. Finally, for the identified NLOS and HCNLOS data, the ranging deviation is reduced using the LightGBM error-correction model, resulting in accurate positioning results. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves a Macro-F1 score of 79.86% for three-class classification in typical indoor scenarios, with positioning root-mean-square errors of 0.0121 m and 0.0142 m in the X and Y directions, respectively.

title: Modeling and Compensation of Multi-Tilt Geometric Errors in a Four-Axis Aero-Engine Blade Measurement System
authors: Jiaxun Sun, Chuanzhi Sun, Zhangqing Yin, Yu Sun, Xin Wang, Yongmeng Liu, Jiubin Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The geometric accuracy of aero-engine blades is critical to engine performance and operational safety, imposing stringent requirements on blade profile measurement precision. This paper presents a three-dimensional blade measurement system based on a four-axis platform equipped with a spectral confocal sensor. To model system-induced geometric deviations, a multi-tilt geometric error model (MT-GEM) is established to jointly account for guide-rail inclination errors, sensor installation errors, and rotary-axis deviations. The MT-GEM is solved in a two-stage manner: initial estimates of the guide-rail inclination parameters are first obtained via ellipsoid fitting combined with linear least-squares estimation, based on which the initial kinematic parameters of the rotary table, including the rotation axis and center, are derived. Subsequently, a joint nonlinear optimization is performed to refine all MT-GEM parameters. To enable efficient calibration with minimal artifacts, a three-parameter constrained sphere pair model (TPCSPM) is introduced, allowing system calibration using only a pair of standard spheres. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves robust and accurate parameter estimation and significantly reduces measurement deviations. After calibration, the radius error of a standard sphere is reduced to 0.018 mm, validating the effectiveness of the proposed method for high-precision, non-contact blade inspection.

title: WAVES: Fine-grained Water Type Mapping via Vision-Language Alignment under Supervision without Pixel-level Labels
authors: Shi Weiyue, Sui Haigang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Fine-grained classification of surface water types is crucial for capturing the functional diversity of aquatic environments, enabling more targeted environmental regulation, hydrological modeling, and land-use planning. However, existing water mapping efforts remain limited to binary segmentation, largely due to the lack of pixel-level annotations and the semantic ambiguity among visually similar water types. In this study, we propose WAVES (Weakly-supervised Alignment for Vision-Language Enhanced Segmentation), a novel framework that combines binary water masks and image-level captions to enable fine-grained water type classification. We design a novel caption-labeling pipeline that combines LLM-generated water-type descriptions with CLIP-based filtering to obtain high-confidence, context-aware supervisory signals. Built upon a CLIP backbone with visual prompt tuning, WAVES incorporates a cross-modal Transformer decoder to produce caption-specific segmentation masks. To bridge the gap between global text and spatial regions, we design a three-part vision-language alignment loss collection, which aligns each caption with its most relevant water region using embedding similarity, enabling water type segmentation without pixel-level annotations. Experiments on the GID dataset show that WAVES achieves a FWIoU of 0.735 from the raw model output, which increases to 0.810 after applying the post-processing module, outperforming both visual-based fully and weakly supervised baselines, especially in complex categories such as aquaculture water and irrigation channels. In a large-scale application in Hubei Province, WAVES maps nine water types classification results across diverse landscapes including agricultural, urban, and mountainous regions. These results demonstrate that WAVES offers a practical solution for detailed water type mapping, capable of handling real-world, large-area applications.

title: An Ultra-Wide Input and Power Range Resonant Converter for Renewable-Fed DC Microgrids with Larger Bus Voltage Fluctuations
authors: Yuanxi Chen, Henry Chung, Hongjian Lin
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: DC microgrids integrating multiple renewable energies such as solar photovoltaic, wind and hydropower typically exhibit bus voltage fluctuations within a wide but coordinated range. Consequently, DC-link applications with dynamic load in DC microgrid require converters with wide input and power operation range. However, the operational range of conventional resonant converters is limited by their fixed resonant LC parameters and restricted switching frequency variation. In this article, a single-stage narrow frequency variation resonant converter featuring a low-cost permeability-controlled variable inductor (PCVI) is proposed to address this issue. The permeability of PCVI is adjusted passively by the operating frequency of the system without the need for external controllers. This alignment allows the frequency changes necessary for gain adjustment in the resonant converter to correspond with the changes of the inductance of PCVI. As the frequency increases, the gain of the resonant converter decreases while the inductance of the PCVI increases, which further reduces the gain. This design minimizes the frequency variation needed for gain regulation and extends the input and power range. Results show that the proposed resonant converter significantly extends the input voltage (60-100V) and power (5-100 W) range compared to conventional solution without requiring external circuits, additional control strategies, and maintaining comparable efficiency at rated conditions.

title: Model Predictive Control of Hybrid Dynamical Systems
authors: Ricardo G. Sanfelice, Berk Altn
abstract: The problem of controlling hybrid dynamical systems using model predictive control (MPC) is formulated and sufficient conditions for asymptotic stability of a set are provided. Hybrid dynamical systems are modeled in terms of hybrid equations, involving a differential equation and a difference equation with inputs and constraints. The proposed hybrid MPC algorithm uses a suitable prediction and control horizon construction inspired by hybrid time domains. Structural properties of the hybrid optimization problem, its feasible set, and its value function are provided. Checkable conditions to guarantee asymptotic stability of a set are provided. These conditions are given in terms of properties on the stage cost, terminal cost, and the existence of static state-feedback laws, related through a control Lyapunov function condition. Examples illustrate the results throughout the paper.

title: Robust Planning for Autonomous Vehicles with Diffusion-Based Failure Samplers
authors: Juanran Wang, Marc R. Schlichting, Mykel J. Kochenderfer
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: High-risk traffic zones such as intersections are a major cause of collisions. This study leverages deep generative models to enhance the safety of autonomous vehicles in an intersection context. We train a 1000-step denoising diffusion probabilistic model to generate collision-causing sensor noise sequences for an autonomous vehicle navigating a four-way intersection based on the current relative position and velocity of an intruder. Using the generative adversarial architecture, the 1000-step model is distilled into a single-step denoising diffusion model which demonstrates fast inference speed while maintaining similar sampling quality. We demonstrate one possible application of the single-step model in building a robust planner for the autonomous vehicle. The planner uses the singlestep model to efficiently sample potential failure cases based on the currently measured traffic state to inform its decisionmaking. Through simulation experiments, the robust planner demonstrates significantly lower failure rate and delay rate compared with the baseline Intelligent Driver Model controller.

title: Techno-Economic Impact of Multi-Frequency Antennas in SATCOM
authors: Mayank, Bisma Manzoor, Tuukka Kuuramaa, Ville Lundén, Milind Thomas Themalil
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: The growing demand for global connectivity driven by 5G, IoT, and mobility calls for flexible satellite platforms supporting multiple simultaneous use cases. Traditional singleband satellites are insufficient, requiring separate missions for diverse frequency needs. This paper presents a techno-economical analysis of emerging multi-frequency antenna architectures for SATCOM (Satellite Communication), addressing onboard space and power limits. We evaluate and compare multi-band reconfigurable antennas, deployable high-gain structures, and metasurface reflectarrays integrated with software-defined radios. A frequency-to-use-case mapping illustrates how spectral agility enables scalable connectivity across government, enterprise, transport, and consumer sectors. Based on a comparative cost model, our analyses of GEO (Geo-stationary Earth Orbit) and MEO (Medium Earth Orbit) platforms show multi-frequency designs can cut CAPEX (Capital Expenditure)/OPEX (Operating Expenditure) by up to 80 %, boosting spectral efficiency and hardware reuse. This study highlights the transformative potential of unified multi-use satellite systems for affordable, inclusive connectivity, especially in underserved regions.

title: Real Time Semantic Segmentation of High Resolution Automotive LiDAR Scans
authors: Hannes Reichert, Benjamin Serfling, Elijah Schüssler, Kerim Turacan, Konrad Doll, Bernhard Sick
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: In recent studies, numerous previous works emphasize the importance of semantic segmentation of LiDAR data as a critical component to the development of driver-assistance systems and autonomous vehicles. However, many state-of-the-art methods are tested on outdated, lower-resolution LiDAR sensors and struggle with real-time constraints. This study introduces a novel semantic segmentation framework tailored for modern high-resolution LiDAR sensors that addresses both accuracy and real-time processing demands. We propose a novel LiDAR dataset collected by a cutting-edge automotive 128 layer LiDAR in urban traffic scenes. Furthermore, we propose a semantic segmentation method utilizing surface normals as strong input features. Our approach is bridging the gap between cutting-edge research and practical automotive applications. Additionaly, we provide a Robot Operating System (ROS2) implementation that we operate on our research vehicle. Our dataset and code are publicly available: https://github.com/kav-institute/SemanticLiDAR. Visual Abstract: Semantic segmentation of a LiDAR scan in RVIZ (ROS2).

title: Detection of Stochastic Orders in the Nonparametric Right Censored Survival Data Model
authors: Tatiana Misharina, Sergey V. Malov
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Information Communication and Software Engineering (ICICSE)
abstract: We create the statistical framework to select and confirm stochastic orders of random variables with a high reliability based on incomplete right censored survival data. The framework is based on the appropriate contrasts created from the corresponding values of survival functions in a certain set of checkpoints. The set checkpoints can be finite or an interval, and can be fixed or obtained from data. We create the hypothesis verification problem, that is closer related to the confidence estimation than to the hypothesis testing problem in sense of statistical conclusions. We use the central limit theorem for the contrasts to confirm one or several appropriate hypotheses on stochastic ordering simultaneously. As a result, we have developed a framework for statistically proving hypotheses on weak stochastic ordering of two or more distributions. Moreover, we analyzed the results of users’ jobs processing at the supercomputer center and established some weak stochastic orders with a given confidence level.

title: InverseLight: Inverse Dynamics Enabling Policy Transfer with Limited Data for Traffic Signal Control
authors: Yang Zhang, Ziyu Bian, Qiyuan Liu, Zhiheng Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Traffic signal control (TSC) plays a vital role in ensuring efficient and safe transportation. While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promising performance in simulators for TSC, transferring these policies to real-world scenarios remains a major challenge due to the dynamics gap and the high cost of collecting large-scale real-world data. In this paper, we propose InverseLight, a two-stage policy transfer framework that enables effective sim-to-real adaptation with limited real-world samples. Our method leverages an inverse dynamics model trained on a small amount of real-world data to reshape the reward function, allowing a pre-trained RL policy to adapt without modifying its architecture. InverseLight is compatible with existing RL-based TSC methods and can be applied as a plug-and-play module. Experimental results on multiple traffic datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces performance degradation during policy transfer, improving traffic efficiency under diverse conditions.

title: Low Power Design Challenges and Trade-Offs in CMOS Image Sensors
authors: Chandani Anand, Sameer Singh, Prashant Govindlal Rupapara, Madhusudan Govindrajan, Manish Goel
publish: 2025 IEEE 6th International Women in Technology Conference (WINTECHCON)
abstract: This paper investigates the primary challenges in designing low-power CMOS image sensor systems, focusing on the trade-offs between power consumption and key performance metrics such as resolution, frame rate, and noise. We propose analog and digital solutions to optimize power efficiency in various operational modes, including full-resolution, binning, skip-sampling, and always-ON modes. A comparative analysis of performance metrics—such as frames-per-second (FPS), resolution, and power consumption—in both active and low-power modes is presented. Our analysis highlight effective strategies for balancing power efficiency with image quality, enabling the development of energy-efficient imaging systems for applications like IoT, mobile phones, and always-on vision sensors.

title: A Resource Scheduling Method Based on the Dual Sparse Attention Mechanism A2C Network
authors: Xueqian Li, Xiuli Yu, Shimin Wei
publish: 2025 13th International Conference on Information and Communication Networks (ICICN)
abstract: To address the challenges of high resource demands and low utilization rates in distributed training resources for current embodied intelligence large model training, a cloud computing resource scheduling method based on a dual sparse attention mechanism A2C network is proposed. This method improves resource scheduling efficiency and load balancing in cloud computing environments through multi-agent collaboration. First, the input task set is classified into four major types according to their characteristics: high-CPU tasks, high-MEM tasks, long-time tasks, and balanced tasks. Second, agents based on the dual sparse attention mechanism A2C network are independently designed according to task characteristics. This method considers dual-objective optimization of scheduling time and load balancing during policy updates. A dual sparse attention mechanism is introduced within the critic network to dynamically screen key information from adjacent agents and achieve implicit collaboration. Finally, simulations were conducted on the Alibaba v2018 dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that compared to Random, FirstFit, and Tetris, the proposed method significantly reduces resource scheduling time, increases throughput, and optimizes load balancing.

title: REFNet++: Multi-Task Efficient Fusion of Camera and Radar Sensor Data in Bird's-Eye Polar View
authors: Kavin Chandrasekaran, Sorin Grigorescu, Gijs Dubbelman, Pavol Jancura
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: A realistic view of the vehicle's surroundings is generally offered by camera sensors, which is crucial for environmental perception. Affordable radar sensors, on the other hand, are becoming invaluable due to their robustness in variable weather conditions. However, because of their noisy output and reduced classification capability, they work best when combined with other sensor data. Specifically, we address the challenge of multimodal sensor fusion by aligning radar and camera data in a unified domain, prioritizing not only accuracy, but also computational efficiency. Our work leverages the raw range-Doppler (RD) spectrum from radar and front-view camera images as inputs. To enable effective fusion, we employ a variational encoder-decoder architecture that learns the transformation of front-view camera data into the Bird's-Eye View (BEV) polar domain. Concurrently, a radar encoder-decoder learns to recover the angle information from the RD data that produce Range-Azimuth (RA) features. This alignment ensures that both modalities are represented in a compatible domain, facilitating robust and efficient sensor fusion. We evaluated our fusion strategy for vehicle detection and free space segmentation against state-of-the-art methods using the RADIal dataset.

title: Patch Exploration-Based Route Planning for Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Huan Meng, Jinhui Zhang, Xiaobing Huang, Ehsan Javanmardi, Manabu Tsukada
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Route planning is a crucial component of autonomous driving, making it essential to develop efficient planning methods tailored to different scenarios. To address the limitations of the existing RRT* methods, we propose a Patch Exploration RRT* (PE-RRT*). First, separating hyperplanes and half-spaces are utilized to construct patch for each node in the tree structure, allowing adaptation to the environmental density. Additionally, during the sampling process, the patches guide the expansion of the random tree, enabling rapid searches and efficient use of sampled points. Moreover, the patches are applied for route pruning, simplifying the route representation. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high search efficiency across various scenarios.

title: Operational Validation of a SWIM-Based UAM Traffic Management System: Lessons from Korea's Grand Challenge
authors: Kwangchun Kang, Suyoung Shin, Hyoungkeun Yoon, Taegyeong Yun, Changhwan Heo
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Urban Air Mobility (UAM) demands new paradigms in traffic management, emphasizing real-time information exchange and coordination among diverse stakeholders. To address these challenges, we developed and field-validated a SWIM-based UAM Traffic Management (UATM) system as part of Korea's K-UAM Grand Challenge Phase 1. The system integrates FF-ICE/FIXM-compliant flight plan management, multi-sensor surveillance fusion, and a modular, service-oriented architecture. Field demonstrations verified the feasibility of SWIM-based interoperability across independently operated systems. This paper discusses key lessons learned regarding data model extensions, sensor integration strategies, and operational scalability, providing practical insights for scalable and resilient UAM deployments.

title: Third Order Dual-Mode Dual Band Half Mode SIW Cavity
authors: Ch. Suresh Kumar, Runa Kumari, Harish.V. Dixit
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents the design of a third-order dual-band Half-Mode Substrate Integrated Waveguide (HMSIW) cavity filter using perturbation technique. The initial HMSIW cavity is designed to resonate at the fundamental TE101 mode at 4.2 GHz. To realize a third-order response, three HMSIW cavities are coupled to reduce the overall footprint. Dual-band is achieved by introducing a slot perturbation in the higher-order TE102 mode, specifically at a location with minimal electric field. Additionally the feed lines are adjusted to introduce transmission zeroes (TZs) to obtain high selectivity and improving isolation between the two pass bands. The resulting dual-band HMSIW filter exhibits resonances at 4.2 GHz and 5.8 GHz, with return losses exceeding 20 dB and insertion losses of 1.1 dB and 1.4 dB, respectively.

title: A Novel Modified Hryvnian Shaped Resonator Loaded Microwave Rotation Sensor for Bio-Medical Motion Tracking and Monitoring
authors: Rachna Prabha, Gujalwar Harsh Vinod, Shivangi Gupta, Prashant Kumar Varshney
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: Accurate measurement of angular motion between body components is crucial in biomedical applications for analyzing human body part movement under medical observation. In this regard, a new type of rotation sensor based on a substrate integrated waveguide (SIW) structure is proposed, loaded with a modified hryvnian shaped resonator for fixed-frequency functionality. The parent SIW is tailored to operate for a fundamental TE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }_{10}$</tex> mode with a cutoff frequency of 6.8 GHz. A modified hryvnian shaped resonator is engraved atop the SIW yielding a transmission zero in the passband of the SIW for the operating mode. The charm of this chosen architecture lies in its fixed frequency operation where the angular movement of the resonator renders resonance notches at a fixed frequency of around 8.5 GHz, thereby mitigating the need for any frequency sweeping device. The designed rotation sensor is very limited in its category as there are only a few fixed frequency sensors available in the literature with compromised performance. The proposed sensor is capable of operating within a range of 70° with excellent linearity and an exceptional sensitivity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.3 ~\text{dB} /{ }^{\circ}$</tex>. It finds potential applicability in biomedical systems for limbs motion detection and other relevant monitoring.

title: Fast Gradient Method (FGM) Fine-Tuning for Bengali Sarcasm Detection
authors: Talha Jobayer Zihan, Mohiuddin Ahmed, A.F.M Minhazur Rahman, Foyez Ahmed Dewan
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer &amp; Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE)
abstract: Language and expression are the way of expressing human emotions, which help humans express feelings and attitudes. However, sarcasm usually masks the true motive, and thus the interpretation of sentiments in online communication is especially difficult. Sarcasm in Bengali is an extremely common culturally sensitive type of humor that can be easily misunderstood on social networks. To overcome this difficulty, we introduce a complete supervised deep learning system in Bengali sarcasm detection, which is based on the BanglaBERT using Fast Gradient Method (FGM) adversarial fine-tuning, layer selective freezing, and regularization optimization. Our experiments on two benchmark datasets, BenSarc and BanglaSarc3, show that our method provides better and more consistent performance than the current semisupervised GAN-BERT architectures while containing a simpler yet more efficient architecture. The proposed model has an accuracy of 77.71% and a weighted F1 score of 0.777 with 50 percent of the data labeled and up to 89.00% accuracy and 0.890 weighted F1 with 100 percent of the data labeled on the BenSarc dataset. Extended to the ternary BanglaSarc3 data set of bilingual Bangla-English inputs and R-Drop regularization, the precision and macro F1 are 82.74% and 0.8275, respectively. These findings point to the fact that a properly optimized supervised BanglaBERT model, enhanced with both adversarial and consistency losses, can be better than semi-supervised alternatives, which is a strong direction to achieve correct sarcasm perception and sentiment detection in low-resource languages such as Bengali.

title: Analysis of Adaptive Antenna System for UAV Based COMINT Payload
authors: Yasho Vijay Singh Yadav, Hariharan Pippari, Adiraju Lakshmi Sowmya, D.B. Dave, T.V.S. Ram, Bharath S.R. Pappula
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents a systems-level analysis of adaptive antenna architectures for airborne Communication Intelligence (COMINT) payloads deployed on high-altitude unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). Operating in the 30 MHz to 3 GHz frequency range, the proposed system leverages geometrically diverse antenna arrays-triangular, square, and hexagonal-with 36 elements to optimize spatial filtering and signal interception capabilities. we demonstrate through simulations that the system achieves a sensitivity of -120 dBm, enabling reliable detection of low-power emitters across wide operational scenarios. The UAV platform, positioned at an altitude of 25 km, offers a ground coverage radius of approximately 567.5 km, significantly enhancing line-of-sight surveillance. Comparative array factor analysis reveals distinct trade-offs in beamwidth, side lobe suppression, and angular resolution across geometries. Additionally, the system incorporates spread spectrum interception capabilities, including Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS) and Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS), to address modern secure and military communication threats. These findings support the deployment of adaptive antenna systems as a scalable and effective solution for next-generation airborne COMINT operations.

title: Revolutionizing Infrastructure Resilience: AI-Driven Approaches to Structural Health Monitoring
authors: Md Zahir Ahmed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Networks and Telecommunications Systems (ANTS)
abstract: Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) ensures the safety and longevity of civil infrastructure. Recent advancements in Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly deep learning (DL), have transformed SHM by enabling automated, scalable solutions for damage detection and assessment. This paper presents a synthesis of AI-driven SHM methods—from vision-based surface damage detection and vibration analysis to physics-informed models and digital twins. We explore the integration of CNNs, RNNs, GANs, and transformers with non-destructive testing (NDT), UAV-based monitoring, and 3D reconstruction. Emphasis is placed on real-time defect localization, sensor fault mitigation, and data recovery in operational environments. Despite progress, challenges like data scarcity, environmental variability, and generalizability remain. We propose a roadmap using hybrid AI-physics models and autonomous SHM systems to enhance infrastructure resilience.

title: Investigation on Printed Circular Loop Antenna and Its Comparison with Annular Ring Monopole Antenna
authors: Ajay Prudhvi Raj Pasupula, K P Ray, BHVS Narayana Murthy
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: This paper presents the design, optimization, and comparative analysis of two compact antenna structures-a circular loop antenna and an annular ring monopole antenna-both operating at 900 MHz on an FR-4 substrate. A novel balanced feeding technique is introduced for the circular loop antenna, employing a parallel wire transmission line (PWTL) for the wire configuration and a parallel strip line (PSL) for the printed version. This approach ensures improved impedance matching with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0}$</tex>-ohm source, enhanced radiation symmetry, and broader impedance bandwidth. In contrast, the annular ring monopole uses a conventional unbalanced microstrip feed with a partial ground plane, typical of monopole designs. Both antennas are simulated and optimized using full-wave electromagnetic analysis in CST Studio Suite. Performance is compared across key metrics including return loss, gain, bandwidth, efficiency, and 2D radiation patterns in both E- and H-planes. Results show that the loop antenna, particularly in its printed PSL-fed form, delivers superior broadside radiation, symmetrical patterns, and wider bandwidth. The study underscores the fundamental differences between magnetic (loop) and electric (monopole) radiators and provides practical insights for antenna design selection. The proposed feeding strategy is validated as an effective solution for developing compact, broadband antennas in wireless, RFID, and IoT applications.

title: Predictive Traffic Control for Evacuation Contraflow Using ZDD Sampling
authors: Satoki Masuda, Eiji Hato
publish: 2025 IEEE 28th International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC)
abstract: Efficient real-time traffic management during evacuations is critical for mitigating the impacts of disasters. This study proposes real-time contraflow operations formulated as a discrete-variable Model Predictive Control (MPC) problem. To address the combinatorial complexity, we develop a novel sampling-based optimization algorithm that integrates Zero-suppressed binary Decision Diagrams (ZDD) with the crossentropy method. The macroscopic fundamental diagram is employed to aggregate zone-based traffic dynamics, enabling rapid state prediction. Numerical experiments show that the ZDD-based sampling achieves 2.2 to 4.7 times faster computation compared to a baseline method. A case study in Tokyo demonstrates that the proposed controller keeps traffic below critical congestion thresholds and improves trip completion by approximately 15 % relative to the scenario without contraflow. These results underscore the potential of discrete-variable MPC with ZDD acceleration as a practical decision-support tool for dynamic traffic management during disasters.

title: Wafer Level Packaging of Quartz Based RF SAW Filters- Process Challenges and Solutions
authors: Kilaru Surendranath, Santanu Sinha, Raj Kumar, Sarode Anuj Ghanshyambhai, Nihar Vaish, Yuvraj Lilhare, K Biju Kumar, Chiranjit Karmakar, Vishwendra Pratap Singh, Jignesh R Bhavsar, R Jesuraja, Shivendra Tripathi, Punam Tyagi
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: Micro-acoustic radio frequency (RF) filters have become the technology of choice for precise frequency band selection in modern mobile communication equipment. Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) and Bulk Acoustic Wave (BAW) are the two variants of this technology that fulfil the filtering requirements up to sub- 6 GHz bands. SAW filters are easier to fabricate, because of lesser number of lithographic mask layer requirements than BAW filters. Quartz and Lithium Tantalate are the most commonly used piezoelectric substrates for SAW filter fabrication. To reduce their form factor, a lot of research has been carried out for developing processes for the Wafer Level Packaging (WLP) of SAW filters. However, most of these efforts, have been directed towards WLP of SAW filters made on Lithium Tantalate. This paper, for the first time, discusses unit processes related to WLP of SAW filters fabricated on Quartz, which is a difficult material to process because of its hardness and chemical inertness. Processes pertaining to deep via etching, via filling and planarization, wafer thinning and polishing, wafer bonding and dicing of bonded wafers are discussed. Challenges in each of these unit process steps are highlighted and ways to overcome them are discussed along with practical results.

title: Flexible Low-Profile Wideband High-Gain Antenna for X Band Applications
authors: Pooja Sharma, Ajay Kumar Poddar, Himanshu Tripathi, Vishal Upmanu, Manju Khari
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: A flexible, low-profile, high-gain rectangular microstrip patch antenna is proposed in this paper for defense applications and wireless communication. This antenna is fabricated on a polyamide substrate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\mathbf{r}}=4.3, \tan \delta=0.004\right)$</tex>. Antenna design consists of a rectangular patch shaped with a defected ground structure with symmetric square slots. Overall dimension of the proposed antenna is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.618 \lambda \times 0.412 \lambda \times 0.0343 \lambda$</tex>. The measured reflection coefficient in free space is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 3 5. 8 ~ d B}$</tex> at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0. 3 G H z}$</tex>. Measured result shows good matching with simulated results and provides a wideband of 4.4 GHz from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9.6-14 \text{GHz}$</tex>. Antenna provides a max. 10 dB gain.

title: Transmission Based Metasurface for Generating OAM Beams
authors: Akash Paramanik, Madasu Venkateswara Rao, Krishnamurthy Chitti Venkata, Subramanian Venkatachalam
publish: 2025 IEEE Microwaves, Antennas, and Propagation Conference (MAPCON)
abstract: Orbital angular momentum (OAM) of electromagnetic (EM) waves is characterized by a helical phase front, which is significant for its potential to enhance communication systems through orthogonal modes. This paper explores the generation of an OAM beam using a transmitting-type metasurface. A unit cell is designed with three copper layers cascaded by two dielectric layers of Rogers RT5880 with a permittivity (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon$</tex>) of 2.2 and a loss tangent <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\tan \delta)$</tex> of 0.0009. This multilayer structure enables a full 360 -degree phase variation for both <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y$</tex>-polarized EM waves, achieved by varying the length of the metallic element in the unit cell. A horn antenna is designed as the feed source for the proposed metasurface, with an optimized focus-to-diameter (F/D) ratio of 0.81. Using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$21 \times 21$</tex> array of unit cells, the transmit array metasurface can generate an OAM beam with modes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$= \pm 1$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 2$</tex> in the 2734 GHz (24.7 % bandwidth) band with a divergence angle of 9.5 and 16 degrees, respectively. Furthermore, the metasurface successfully generates OAM beams with a high mode purity for both polarizations.

title: Data-driven Remaining Useful Life Prediction for Optical Modules in Intelligent Computing Networks
authors: Ningchun Liu, Aiyue Wang, Anyi Tang, Geyang Xiao, Jun Zhu, Tao Zou
publish: 2025 Global Reliability and Prognostics and Health Management Conference (PHM-Xian)
abstract: The stability of optical modules significantly impacts overall system performance in intelligent computing networks. Failures in optical modules can lead to a series of issues, diminishing the operational efficiency of intelligent computing centers. Therefore, accurately predicting the remaining useful life of optical modules is crucial for maintaining network stability and minimizing maintenance costs. Currently, traditional physics-based prediction methods rely heavily on precise parameters and are therefore unsuitable for complex intelligent computing network environments. In contrast, existing data-driven prediction models typically use simulated or small-scale experimental data, resulting in limited model generalization capabilities.
